title: Add SLO→Customer Impact Classifier & Remediation Workflow doc (v2 scoring + updated comms)
preview:
Context
We have an internal Confluence page describing the SLO→customer impact tier classifier and the remediation workflow. That page is being referenced during incidents, but the operational details have drifted since the enrichment pipeline shipped score weighting changes (v2) and CS escalation timing was tightened after SUP-19344.

This PR adds the canonical doc to the slo-toolkit repo so it can be versioned alongside the classifier code and reviewed via normal PR workflows. The majority of the content mirrors the Confluence page, but includes the v2 scoring thresholds and updated templates.

What changed (summary)
- Keep: tier definitions (1–4), overall workflow (alert→enrich→score→tier→remediate), evidence retention requirements, and post-incident/error budget reconciliation flow.
- Update: score mapping thresholds (v2), add confidence gating and a human-review fallback, update Tier 1 comms SLA (10 min), and reduce Tier 4 auto-escalation window (4 hours).
- Update: SLO catalog excerpt targets to match current baselines; add explicit note that targets are examples and owners must use the live SLO registry as source of truth.
- Update: capacity knobs table to reflect current CLI commands and pool naming.

Doc content (added under docs/incident-response/)

Title: SLO to Customer Impact Classifier and Remediation Workflow

Overview
This document defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 250ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defined rolling window.
- Customer-impact tier: a 1–4 classification used to route and prioritize incident response.

Customer-impact tiers (quick guide)
| Tier | User-visible symptoms | Business effect | Escalation urgency | Owner to notify |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Multi-region outage OR widespread request failures OR sustained elevated error rate affecting a large portion of traffic | Severe revenue/customer retention impact | Immediate (page) | SRE lead + Product PM + CS Director |
| 2 | Single-region outage, or large subset of customers impacted (major tenants) or major feature down | Significant business disruption for key customers | High (page + slack) | SRE oncall + Product owner + CS manager |
| 3 | Localized errors, degraded latency for non-critical endpoints | Minor customer annoyance, limited support tickets | Medium (slack alert) | On-call SRE + Feature owner |
| 4 | Minor latency increase or transient errors with no customer tickets | No immediate business impact | Low (ticket) | On-call SRE |

SLO catalog excerpt (example mapping; owners must confirm in live SLO registry)
| SLO name | Metric | Target | Error-budget window | Default customer-impact tier |
|----------|--------|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| request_latency_p95_public | request_latency_p95{route=~"/v1/.*"} | <= 250ms | 30d | 2 |
| api_error_rate_5m | sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) | <= 0.5% | 7d | 1 |
| embedding_throughput_tail | embedding_infer_latency_p99 | < 1.4s | 14d | 3 |
| console_ui_availability | synthetic_check(console_login) | 99.7% monthly | 30d | 3 |

Classification algorithm (operational)
1) Alert receives telemetry: detector alerts on SLI breach or error-budget burn (alerting config).
2) Metrics enrichment: pull impact signals (affected tenant count, region distribution, top N tenants by RPS, open support tickets matching pattern).
3) Heuristics engine evaluates scores (0–100): failure-scope, severity, customer-exposure.
4) Confidence gating (v2): compute confidence (0–100) based on completeness/consistency of enrichment inputs. If confidence < 50, do not auto-upgrade beyond Tier 3; route to on-call for manual triage and re-run enrichment after 5 minutes.
5) Combined score maps to Tier (v2 thresholds): Score >= 85 -> Tier 1; 70–84 -> Tier 2; 35–69 -> Tier 3; < 35 -> Tier 4.
6) Attach classification + confidence to incident and follow the tiered remediation workflow below.

Heuristics inputs (examples)
- affected_tenant_ratio: percent of total tenant RPS impacted.
- p95_latency_delta: percent change vs baseline.
- error_rate_delta: absolute change in error rate vs baseline (preferred over p99_error_spike for API).
- active_cs_tickets: number of open tickets tagged /inference or /api.
- region_blast_radius: number of regions with concurrent impact.

Operational remediations by tier
Tier 1 (Immediate response)
- Page the SRE oncall and SRE lead (PagerDuty service: customer-impact).
- Open an incident in the incident-management system using template tag=CUSTOMER_IMPACT_TIER_1 (auto-created if classifier confidence >= 70).
- Run mitigation playbook: fallback-to-compatible-model-variant.
- If capacity-related: trigger immediate capacity burst policy (see CLI below).
- Notify CS and Product leadership within 10 minutes with templated Slack/Jira.

Tier 2 (High-priority)
- Page on-call (high urgency) and post to #incidents.
- Execute targeted throttles or tenant fair-share configuration.
- Begin canary rollback of recent model or config changes (if correlated).

Tier 3 (Operational handling)
- Create ticket and schedule on-call investigation within 30–60 minutes.
- Apply mitigations from the runbook: restart worker pool, clear KV cache, or disable newly-deployed feature flag.

Tier 4 (Monitoring)
- Monitor metrics and let automated remediation run. If unresolved after SLA window (4 hours), escalate to Tier 3.

Sample alert rule (Prometheus-style YAML)
alert: HighAPIErrorRate
expr: sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.005
for: 2m
labels: severity: page impact: "possible-customer-impact"
annotations: summary: "API error rate >0.5% over 5m" runbook: "https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation"

Enrichment query examples (pseudo-SQL / analytics)
- Tenants affected in last 10m: SELECT tenant_id, count(*) FROM requests WHERE status >= 500 AND timestamp > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 50;
- Support ticket correlation: count tickets where created_at >= now()-10m AND tags IN ('api','inference')

Capacity escalation knobs (examples)
| Trigger | Condition | Automated action | Escalation threshold |
|---------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| headroom_exhausted | available_gpu_slots < 7% | trigger-burst-alloc --pool=shared-burst-v2 | immediate (Tier1) |
| model_warm_pool_empty | warm_pool_size < configured_min | scale warm pool by +2 nodes | Tier2 |
| p95_latency_spike | p95 > baseline*1.6 for 10m | enable-prefetch-kv-cache=true | Tier2 |

CLI examples
- Trigger burst capacity (operators only): rcmd infra capacity burst apply --pool shared-burst-v2 --reason "Tier1 SLO breach" --expires 30m
- Re-route traffic to fallback models: rcmd infra routing promote --from model-xyz-v2 --to model-xyz-v1 --weight 100

Communication templates
Slack template (Tier1)
- Channel: #incidents
- Message: [Tier 1 SLO breach] api_error_rate_5m at 1.2% impacting ~32% of tenant RPS across 2 regions. IC: @oncall. Mitigation in progress: promoting fallback routing. Please notify CS lead @renee.zhao. Incident: <INCIDENT_URL>

Jira template fields
- Priority: P0 for Tier1, P1 for Tier2, P2 for Tier3/4
- Components: runtime, routing, capacity, product-feature
- Labels: slo-breach, customer-impact-tier-<n>, automated-enrichment, classifier-v2

Post-incident / Error-budget reconciliation
1) Add classification evidence (metrics, tenant list, tickets) to the incident story.
2) Compute error-budget consumption attributable to the incident window.
3) For Tier1/Tier2 incidents: schedule a joint SRE/Product/CS triage within 72 hours. The triage determines whether to take a governance action (e.g., customer credits, temporary throttle, emergency capacity commit).
4) Document remediation plan and assign owners with due dates.

Postmortem checklist (to include in incident review)
- Summary of classifier result (tier + confidence) and whether the initial tier matched customer reports.
- Timeline of detection -> mitigation -> recovery.
- Root cause and contributing factors including capacity and SLO misconfigurations.
- Action items (owner + due date) and follow-up on error-budget policy.
- Evidence attachments: metrics, logs, enrichment query results.

Automation and operational tooling notes
- The classifier runs in the telemetry enrichment pipeline (owner: telemetry-team). Inputs are Prometheus alerts, request traces, and support ticket API.
- All automated classification decisions must be auditable: store computed scores, confidence, and input summaries in incident metadata.
- Confidence gating exists to prevent false Tier 1/2 upgrades when enrichment is degraded (e.g., ticket API latency, tenant-mapping lag).

Ownership and governance
- SLO owner: Each SLO must list a primary owner (SRE or Product) and a secondary owner. Owners are responsible for maintaining mapping in the SLO registry and ensuring this doc stays consistent.
- Classification engine owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com.
- Incident review owner for Tier1/Tier2: SRE lead on rotation.

Runbook links (examples)
- API error mitigation: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation
- Fallback routing: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/fallback-routing-and-rollbacks
- Capacity burst playbook: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/capacity-burst-and-burst-rollback

Change log
- 2026-01-15 — Initial draft published (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-20 — Added capacity knobs and CLI examples (review: Liam O'Connor).
- 2026-03-01 — Finalized communication templates and post-incident checklist (review: Renee Zhao).
- 2026-03-16 — Updated classifier thresholds to v2, added confidence gating, tightened Tier1 notification SLA to 10 min, and reduced Tier4 escalation window to 4 hours (review: Sasha Kim).

Appendix: sample incident evidence payload (illustrative)
- incident_id=INC-2026-0316-014 slo=api_error_rate_5m failure_scope=81 severity=88 customer_exposure=63 confidence=77 mapped_tier=1 top_affected_tenants=[tenant-acme,tenant-vela] evidence_queries=[tenant_impact_query_2026-03-16-10m]

Operator note
If you are an on-call SRE and this workflow appears to misclassify an incident, create a ticket in the SRE backlog and tag telemetry-team for review. Include the incident link and the classifier scores/confidence from incident metadata.

Contact
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.com)
- Telemetry owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com
- Incident management: incidents@redwood.com

Testing
- N/A (documentation only).

Rollout
- Once merged, we will link this doc from the internal runbook index and update the Confluence page to point at this repo copy as the source of truth.

title: add content-disposition/metadata guards and per-tool payload quotas for multipart uploads
preview: Motivation: Tool/file attachments used by the OpenAI-compatible tool-calling surface were allowing poorly formed multipart uploads and oversized payloads which caused streaming stalls and OOMs in the serving runtime. This PR introduces explicit guards around Content-Disposition and file metadata, per-tool and per-account payload quotas for multipart/form-data parts, improved multipart boundary handling to avoid partial-read hangs in stream mode, and small compatibility tweaks so the OpenAI compatibility layer surfaces a stable `files` metadata object for tool calls. Changes are intentionally conservative: they add validation and soft-failure modes (400 + diagnostics) by default and an env flag to opt into hard rejection for stricter enterprise deployments. The description below summarizes goals, changes, test coverage, and rollout plan.

title: Proactive tail harmonizer and cohort splitter for longitudinal benchmarks
preview:
Context and motivation: Longitudinal benchmark runs at Redwood have shown unstable tail behavior when cohorts drift across multiple phases (warmup -> steady -> degraded). This PR introduces a lightweight 'Proactive Tail Harmonizer' that proactively detects cohort drift and a deterministic 'Cohort Splitter' that partitions sessions into stable cohorts for more reproducible long-tail quantiles. The goal is to reduce p99/p999 variance across repeated runs and make performance changes easier to attribute to code or config changes.

What this PR delivers (summary): 1) An in-process harmonizer module that computes cohort fingerprints over sliding windows and applies a corrective reweighting when cohort composition shifts by >5% for the tail cohort. 2) A deterministic cohort splitter that marks sessions into stable buckets based on request size distribution, KV cache hit patterns, and primer length. 3) A downstream exporter that annotates existing benchmark histograms with cohort tags so downstream analysis can isolate regressions per cohort. 4) Integration test harness + a new smoke profile that reproduces a previously non-deterministic p999 spike locally.

Design notes (high level): - Harmonizer runs as a lightweight sampler on the bench agent; it maintains a compact Bloom-filter style fingerprint for session features (sequence length histogram, KV-hit pattern signature, primer tokens). - When the harmonizer detects a drift, it emits a reweight instruction to the workload generator to over-sample or under-sample specific cohorts for the remainder of the epoch. This is a corrective step (not blocking) to prevent single-cohort dominance. - Cohort Splitter uses deterministic hashing on normalized feature vectors to guarantee repeatability across runs; the mapping is stable unless the feature normalization changes. - Exporter updates the telemetry envelope with `cohort_id` and `cohort_health` fields so existing dashboards can be filtered with minimal changes.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of harmonizer and splitter modules
- [x] Unit tests for fingerprinting and deterministic hashing
- [x] Integration smoke that reproduces flaky p999 (bench-suite/fixtures/p999-flaky.json)
- [x] Telemetry exporter annotations and docs snippet
- [x] CI passing after flaky test rerun

Testing and validation:
- Ran 120 repeated runs against the local mini-cluster; variance in p999 decreased from 28% to 6% across the canonical seq-mix workload. Numbers and raw traces are attached to ENG-892.
- Verified no measurable penalty to median latency (p50 change < 1ms) in steady-state.
- CI initially flagged a flaky integration test (bench-integration::session-replay) — root cause was a nondeterministic seed in the fixture; fixed by aligning seeds in the smoke profile.

Backward compatibility and rollout:
- Harmonizer default is off; feature flag `bench.harmonizer.enabled` is set to false in main. The PR includes a rollout plan to enable in canary runs for one week before broader baseline updates.
- Cohort Splitter is safe to enable by default because mapping is deterministic; warning added to docs about changing normalization.

Related: ENG-892 (investigation), ENG-904 (dashboard followup), docs PR will follow to redwood-docs.

Files touched (high-level):
- bench-agent/harmonizer/harmonizer.go -- core fingerprinting and drift detector
- bench-agent/harmonizer/fingerprint_test.go -- unit tests
- bench-generator/cohort_splitter.go -- deterministic hashing + sampling control hooks
- bench-exporter/cohort_annotations.go -- telemetry annotation logic and serializer
- bench-suite/profiles/p999-smoke.json -- smoke profile that triggers the spike used for validation
- bench-integration/fixtures/p999-flaky.json -- fixed nondeterministic seed
- bench-ci/workflows/harmonizer-smoke.yml -- workflow to run smoke in PRs

Commits (summary):
- init: add harmonizer core and interfaces
- tests: add fingerprint unit tests and fixtures
- feature: implement cohort splitter and generator hooks
- exporter: annotate telemetry with cohort_id and cohort_health
- ci: add smoke workflow and fix seeds in fixture

Change rationale: The lab's previous approach relied on passive post-hoc cohort tagging which made attribution noisy; this PR moves the lab to proactive stabilization so regressions affecting small cohorts don't get masked by run-to-run cohort shifts.

Rollout plan (short): stage -> canary (2 weeks with harmonizer enabled) -> baseline update with cohort-aware histograms -> enable exporter everywhere.

Notes: This PR purposefully keeps harmonizer heuristics conservative; thresholds are feature-flagged for experimentation. Documentation and dashboards updates are tracked in ENG-904.

This is a corrective step (not blocking) to prevent single-cohort dominance. - Cohort Splitter uses deterministic hashing on normalized feature vectors to guarantee repeatability across runs; the mapping is stable unless the feature normalization changes. - Exporter updates the telemetry envelope with `cohort_id` and `cohort_health` fields so existing dashboards can be filtered with minimal changes.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of harmonizer and splitter modules
- [x] Unit tests for fingerprinting and deterministic hashing
- [x] Integration smoke that reproduces flaky p999 (bench-suite/fixtures/p999-flaky.json)
- [x] Telemetry exporter annotations and docs snippet
- [x] CI passing after flaky test rerun

Testing and validation:
- Ran 120 repeated runs against the local mini-cluster; variance in p999 decreased from 28% to 6% across the canonical seq-mix workload. Numbers and raw traces are attached to ENG-892.
- Verified no measurable penalty to median latency (p50 change < 1ms) in steady-state.
- CI initially flagged a flaky integration test (bench-integration::session-replay) — root cause was a nondeterministic seed in the fixture; fixed by aligning seeds in the smoke profile.

Backward compatibility and rollout:
- Harmonizer default is off; feature flag `bench.harmonizer.enabled` is set to false in main. The PR includes a rollout plan to enable in canary runs for one week before broader baseline updates.
- Cohort Splitter is safe to enable by default because mapping is deterministic; warning added to docs about changing normalization.

Related: ENG-892 (investigation), ENG-904 (dashboard followup), docs PR will follow to redwood-docs.

- bench-agent/harmonizer/harmonizer.go -- core fingerprinting and drift detector
- bench-agent/harmonizer/fingerprint_test.go -- unit tests
- bench-generator/cohort_splitter.go -- deterministic hashing + sampling control hooks
- bench-exporter/cohort_annotations.go -- telemetry annotation logic and serializer

Change rationale: The lab's previous approach relied on passive post-hoc cohort tagging which made attribution noisy; this PR moves the lab to proactive stabilization so regressions affecting small cohorts don't get masked by run-to-run cohort shifts.

title: Outlier-aware autoscaler and temporal capacity hedge for SLO fencing
preview:
Motivation: production clusters occasionally see short-lived, high-intensity request bursts from a small subset of tenants (outliers) that cause noisy autoscaler signals and suboptimal capacity allocation. This PR introduces an outlier-aware autoscaling component combined with a temporal capacity hedge that absorbs transient spikes while preserving throughput guarantees for steady tenants.

What this PR does: Adds an Outlier Detector that maintains lightweight per-tenant percentile sketches (latency + qps) and flags tenants with sudden delta > configurable threshold. When outliers are detected, the autoscaler engages a Temporal Hedge window (soft buffer of reserved ephemeral slots) and a SLO-fencing policy that temporarily isolates the outlier's burst from normal reservation accounting. The Hedge uses exponential decay of reserved capacity over a sliding window (configurable half-life) to avoid long tail reservation leaks.

Design highlights: - Outlier Detector: lightweight C++ component in runtime/allocator that updates P2/TDigest-like sketches and emits outlier events to the autoscaler controller. - Temporal Hedge: controller-managed soft reservoir scoped per-pool with leak (decay) and forgiveness window parameters. - SLO Fencing: a policy layer that enforces per-tenant in-flight concurrency caps during hedge engagement and triggers backoff advisories to gateway clients. - Telemetry: new utilization histogram series (per-pool and per-tenant sampled) and outlier event metrics, plus a tracer span for hedge lifecycle. - Safety: fails closed — if the detector crashes, autoscaler falls back to existing smoothing logic.

Checklist: [x] design doc referenced (Linear ENG-2314) [x] unit tests for detector logic [x] e2e sim in benchmark-lab harness [x] telemetry dashboards updated [x] rollout/feature-flag gating implemented

Related tickets: ENG-2314, ENG-2397, PM-412.

Implementation notes: - Runtime changes are confined to runtime/allocator/outlier_detector.{cc,h} and runtime/controller/hedge_controller.cc. - Controller exposes two new flags (hedge_window_ms, hedge_decay_half_life_ms) and a per-tenant manual override label. - Gateway side advisory implemented via an advisory header (x-redwood-hedge-suggested-backoff) for best-effort client guidance.

Testing: included unit tests in runtime/tests and controller/tests, plus an integration scenario in benchmark-lab that runs a 5k qps baseline with injected tenant spikes; validated hedge absorbs bursts with <3% SLO violations and reduces churn in scale events by ~48%.

Rollout plan: staged rollout behind feature flag outlier-hedge. Canary on two dedicated fleets for 48h -> expand to 25% of fleets for 72h -> global. Observability guardrails: increase histogram retention during rollouts; automatic rollback if 95th latency regression >10% over baseline for 3 consecutive windows.

Notes on operator UX: added a console page (redwood-console -> Capacity -> Temporal Hedges) with current hedges, their decay windows, and manual drain controls.

title: introduce-sse-serde-sanitizer-and-ordered-tool-handoff
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see rare incidents where SSE consumers receive partial JSON payloads that are syntactically valid individually but not reconstructible into the originally intended structured output due to delimiter coalescing and late-arriving fragments. These failures surface as missing tool/function call handoffs or JSON parse errors in downstream SDKs (notably in OpenAI compatibility layer consumers). This PR adds a lightweight 'SSE serde sanitizer' on both emitter and parser sides to ensure atomic span re-emission semantics and deterministic ordered handoff for tool/function calls.

Summary of changes: Adds a boundary-collation pass during SSE emission that tags span segments with a stable span-id and an ordinal within-span index. On the parser side we use the span-id + ordinal to: 1) detect gaps (missing ordinal sequence), 2) temporarily stash out-of-order segments, 3) apply a short reassembly backoff window to allow late-arriving fragments to join the ordered stream, and 4) when backoff expires, emit a deterministic "reemit" envelope that contains an escape-wrapped partial JSON with a safe sentinel so downstream structured-output handlers can either attempt best-effort merge or surface a clear, actionable parse error.

Why this is different from previous work: Prior PRs focused on delimiter normalization and naive reassembly heuristics that attempted byte-level coalescing. This change introduces semantic span metadata (span-id + ordinal) and an explicit fallback reemit envelope rather than silent byte concatenation — helping SDKs make informed choices (retry, fallback model, or fail fast). It also introduces an ordered tool handoff marker (tool-ordinal) to guarantee function-call ordering even when chunks interleave across connections.

High-level risk and rollout plan: The new span-id is opt-in on the emitter via a feature flag; readers treat untagged streams as pre-2026 format and use legacy heuristics. We will roll out on hosted API first behind the flag, run Canary for two days, then flip default-on after monitoring for a week. The parser includes a compact compatibility mode so older self-hosted Private deployments can accept the new envelopes without interface changes.

Backwards-compat notes: SDKs that parse streaming structured output should be updated to recognize the reemit envelope and the tool-ordinal marker; fallbacks are documented in the release notes. The compatibility layer will continue emitting the plain SSE lines if the emitter configuration disables span tagging.

title: Multi-locale RAG quickstart and i18n evaluation playbook
Introduce region-assertion propagator and signed-audit-proof headers
preview:
Background and motivation: A recent support escalation (SUP-442) and an internal incident showed intermittent KMS calls routed outside the tenant's allowed region when requests were proxied through legacy gateways that dropped the tenant-region claim. This change adds a lightweight propagator that ensures a cryptographically signed region assertion (region-assertion and audit-proof) travels with requests from the API gateway through service mesh to the KMS plugin and audit-forwarder. The goal is to make KMS region checks and audit evidence verification robust even when intermediate proxies sanitize standard headers.

What this PR does:
- Introduces a RegionAssertionPropagator component in the api-gateway that signs a compact assertion (tenant_id, region, issued_at, nonce, signature) using the platform signing key and attaches it as X-Redwood-AuditProof.
- Extends the auth-service to surface canonical tenant claims (tenant_id, allowed_regions) in a machine-readable JSON Web Claim (not a JWT) consumed by downstream services.
- Adds validation hooks in the kms-plugin to prefer region information from X-Redwood-AuditProof if present, falling back to the existing tenant claim only when the header is absent.
- Enhances audit-forwarder to store the audit-proof header alongside existing envelopes for tamper-evident retention and debugability.
- Adds a small translation layer in rbac-policy-compiler that maps older region-label policies to the new assertion model during a rollout window.
- End-to-end test coverage: new e2e scenario that simulates a proxy scrubbing standard headers but preserving the audit-proof header to verify KMS calls remain region-locked.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc reviewed by Security and Infra (ENG-8373)
- [x] Unit tests for propagator signing and verification
- [x] Integration tests for gateway->service->kms path
- [x] e2e test for proxy-scrub scenario
- [x] Feature gate implemented (rbac.v2.region_propagation) and config defaults to off for Dedicated tenants
- [x] Rolling rollout plan and monitoring dashboards updated

Changes (high-level):
Commits:
- feat(region): add RegionAssertionPropagator to api-gateway (Asha K)
- feat(auth): expose canonical tenant claims endpoint and library (Mateo A)
- fix(kms-plugin): prefer audit-proof header for region enforcement (Evan C)
- feat(audit): persist audit-proof header in forwarder envelopes (Lena P)
- feat(rbac-compiler): add translation shim for legacy region labels (Asha K)
- test(e2e): add proxy-scrub scenario to canary suite (QA)
- test(e2e): add proxy-scrub scenario to canary suite (QA)
- chore: gofmt and minor lint fixes (QA)

Files touched (high level):
- services/api-gateway/src/propagator/region_propagator.go
- services/auth-service/src/claims/canonical_claims.go
- infra/kms-plugin/src/region_enforcer.go
- infra/audit-forwarder/src/envelope_store.go
- infra/rbac-compiler/src/translator/region_shim.go
- tests/e2e/proxy_scrub_scenario.yaml
- docs/security/region-propagation.md (new)

Notable implementation details:
- The audit-proof header is intentionally compact (base64 of JSON + ed25519 signature) to avoid hitting header size limits and to be resilient across proxies.
- Signing keys are read from the existing platform KMS with a transient ephemeral signing key cached per process. No long-lived private keys are introduced.
- The propagator runs as a filter in api-gateway and only emits the header if the tenant has allowed_regions configured; otherwise it omits it to avoid leakage.
- Validation in kms-plugin includes nonce replay protection backed by a short-lived in-memory cache and a secondary async dedupe check in Dynamo for cross-process safety.

Review conversation (highlights):
- Lena Park (review): "Can the signing path fall back gracefully if the platform signing KMS is temporarily unavailable? We don't want to block requests."
- Asha (reply): "Propagator will fall back to emitting the unsigned claim as X-Redwood-AuditProof-V1 (unverified). KMS enforcer rejects unverified claims by default; this behavior is documented and gated. I've added metrics to signal unsigned emissions so we can monitor and escalate."
- Evan Cho (review request changes): "Add unit tests for nonce replay across goroutines and a failure-mode integration test where signer throws transient errors."
- Asha (reply): "Added tests and increased retry window. See commits feat(region) and test(e2e)."
- Mateo Alvarez (approval): "Verified logic in kms-plugin; translation shim is safe for rollout. Approving."

CI summary: Initial CI run: build passed, unit tests passed, integration suite failed on the e2e proxy-scrub test (timeout) on first run. Flaky e2e was re-run and passed on second attempt. Linting: minor gofmt fixes applied in a follow-up commit. Security scans: SAST flagged use of base64/json concatenation for header formatting as potential canonicalization risk; addressed by adding explicit canonical JSON marshalling and tests.

Monitoring and rollout notes:
- Feature gate rbac.v2.region_propagation default: off in production for two weeks; enable in canary region for 48 hours with increased audit retention and a replay detector alert.
- New dashboards: km.core.region_assertion.{signed,unsigned} and kms.enforcer.rejects.by_reason
- Alert: >5% unsigned assertions in canary region for 10m triggers pager.

Migration/compatibility:
- rbac-compiler translation shim automatically maps older region label rules to accept the new header during the rollout window; not intended as permanent.
- No database schema changes.
- Backwards compatibility: services that do not understand X-Redwood-AuditProof continue to use canonical tenant claims.

Testing notes:
- Unit tests added for signer/verifier and nonce replay protection.
- Integration tests added for gateway->service->kms happy path and fail-open/fail-closed scenarios.
- e2e scenario simulates an intermediate proxy that strips Authorization and standard tenant headers but preserves X-Redwood-AuditProof.

Merge and release:
- Merge method: squash and merge on main
- Merged by: Lena Park after approvals and green CI.
- Rollout ticket: ENG-8373 (contains runbook and rollback steps)

Follow-ups (planned):
- Instrument cross-region audit-proof dedupe visibility (SEC-211)
- Add support for rotating signing keys and key id metadata on header
- Add small docs and SDK helpers for types in redwood-sdk-go and redwood-sdk-python to make adoption easier in partner infra.

Author notes: This PR intentionally keeps the assertion format minimal to accelerate adoption, and provides a clear upgrade path for services. The feature is gated and covered by e2e to reduce blast-radius. If we want to make the header fully required, follow-up work will strengthen cross-process nonce persistence and increase retention on audit indexes.

title: token-cost-delta-heatmaps-and-auto-tune-alert-curves
preview:
Motivation: We currently surface per-token cost and latency aggregates, but teams continue to hit noisy alerts driven by short-lived spikes and non-actionable single-bucket anomalies. This PR introduces a token-cost delta surface (heatmaps of recent delta distributions), a smoothing/decay-based alert curve tuner, and dashboard widgets to help SREs and product engineers see whether cost regressions are transient or systemic.

Summary of changes: 1) New metric family token_cost.delta.{route,model,tenant} capturing short-window (1m/5m/15m) relative delta compared to a rolling baseline; uses reservoir decaying sketch to bound cardinality. 2) Heatmap rollup job that produces downsampled 2D histograms for UI rendering and retention-friendly rollups. 3) Alert curve component: alert rules now accept a tunable slope/decay parameter and a small Bayesian prior to reduce flapping; added auto-tune controller that adjusts thresholds based on recent delta quantiles and SLO burnrate. 4) Dashboard: new Observability > Token Cost Delta page with interactive heatmap, recent anomalous routes table, and quick links to rerun evals. 5) Small runtime export hook to tag spans with delta-bucket and tuned-alert-version for trace correlation.

Why this is different: instead of relying solely on percentiles, the delta heatmap surface captures the shape of the change across token-position and time, enabling targeted mitigations (cache warmups, batching changes, model fallback). Auto-tune reduces alert noise by learning stable thresholds per-tenant and per-route while preserving sensitivity to regressions.

Checklist:
- [x] Metric instrumentation and exporter (server + ingestion)
- [x] Heatmap rollup and retention policy
- [x] Alert curve and auto-tune controller
- [x] Console dashboard and widgets
- [x] Tracing span enrichment and correlation keys
- [x] Unit and integration tests for controller logic

Commits summary: 
- feat(obs): add token_cost.delta metrics and reservoir-decay sketch implementation
- feat(obs): heatmap rollup worker and storage schema for downsampled histograms
- feat(console): token-cost-delta dashboard and heatmap widget
- feat(alerts): alert-curve schema + auto-tune controller and CLI trainer
- fix(tests): stabilize timing in controller unit tests

Files changed (high-level): 
- pkg/observability/metrics/delta.go (new)
- pkg/observability/rollup/heatmap_worker.go (new)
- pkg/observability/storage/schema.sql (migration)
- internal/alerts/curve_tuner.go (new)
- internal/alerts/rules.yaml (updated)
- web/console/src/pages/TokenCostDeltaPage.tsx (new)
- web/console/src/components/HeatmapChart.tsx (new)
- server/trace/span_enricher.go (small hook)
- tests/observability/delta_controller_test.go (new)

Notes on implementation details: token_cost.delta is implemented as a relative delta = (window_avg - rolling_baseline)/max(rolling_baseline, epsilon) to keep values normalized across tenants. The reservoir-decay sketch binds memory while allowing quantile approximation for each route: we found 2048 items per-route to be a practical default after benchmarking on internal traces. Heatmap rollup encodes 2D histograms in a compact protobuf and stores daily rollups with linear downsampling for 90-day retention. The auto-tune controller uses an adjustable prior (default 0.02) so early noisy tenants do not set overly sensitive thresholds.

Backward compatibility and migration: The migration adds a new storage table but does not change existing metric names. Alert rule updates are additive and kept behind a feature flag `token_cost_delta_alerts`. Rolling migration plan in PR description for operators is included in the console doc link.

Operational considerations: Added a feature flag and a migration script (pkg/observability/storage/migrations/20260214_add_delta.sql) and a runbook entry for SREs in docs/runbooks/token-cost-delta.md describing how to revert tuning, inspect heatmaps, and run offline calibration.

text:
Motivation: We currently surface per-token cost and latency aggregates, but teams continue to hit noisy alerts driven by short-lived spikes and non-actionable single-bucket anomalies. This PR introduces a token-cost delta surface (heatmaps of recent delta distributions), a smoothing/decay-based alert curve tuner, and dashboard widgets to help SREs and product engineers see whether cost regressions are transient or systemic.

Summary of changes: 1) New metric family token_cost.delta.{route,model,tenant} capturing short-window (1m/5m/15m) relative delta compared to a rolling baseline; uses reservoir decaying sketch to bound cardinality. 2) Heatmap rollup job that produces downsampled 2D histograms for UI rendering and retention-friendly rollups. 3) Alert curve component: alert rules now accept a tunable slope/decay parameter and a small Bayesian prior to reduce flapping; added auto-tune controller that adjusts thresholds based on recent delta quantiles and SLO burnrate. 4) Dashboard: new Observability > Token Cost Delta page with interactive heatmap, recent anomalous routes table, and quick links to rerun evals. 5) Small runtime export hook to tag spans with delta-bucket and tuned-alert-version for trace correlation.

Why this is different: instead of relying solely on percentiles, the delta heatmap surface captures the shape of the change across token-position and time, enabling targeted mitigations (cache warmups, batching changes, model fallback). Auto-tune reduces alert noise by learning stable thresholds per-tenant and per-route while preserving sensitivity to regressions.

Checklist:
- [x] Metric instrumentation and exporter (server + ingestion)
- [x] Heatmap rollup and retention policy
- [x] Alert curve and auto-tune controller
- [x] Console dashboard and widgets
- [x] Tracing span enrichment and correlation keys
- [x] Unit and integration tests for controller logic

Commits summary: 
- feat(obs): add token_cost.delta metrics and reservoir-decay sketch implementation
- feat(obs): heatmap rollup worker and storage schema for downsampled histograms
- feat(console): token-cost-delta dashboard and heatmap widget
- feat(alerts): alert-curve schema + auto-tune controller and CLI trainer
- fix(tests): stabilize timing in controller unit tests

Files changed (high-level): 
- pkg/observability/metrics/delta.go (new)
- pkg/observability/rollup/heatmap_worker.go (new)
- pkg/observability/storage/schema.sql (migration)
- internal/alerts/curve_tuner.go (new)
- internal/alerts/rules.yaml (updated)
- web/console/src/pages/TokenCostDeltaPage.tsx (new)
- web/console/src/components/HeatmapChart.tsx (new)
- server/trace/span_enricher.go (small hook)
- tests/observability/delta_controller_test.go (new)

Notes on implementation details: token_cost.delta is implemented as a relative delta = (window_avg - rolling_baseline)/max(rolling_baseline, epsilon) to keep values normalized across tenants. The reservoir-decay sketch binds memory while allowing quantile approximation for each route: we found 2048 items per-route to be a practical default after benchmarking on internal traces. Heatmap rollup encodes 2D histograms in a compact protobuf and stores daily rollups with linear downsampling for 90-day retention. The auto-tune controller uses an adjustable prior (default 0.02) so early noisy tenants do not set overly sensitive thresholds.

Backward compatibility and migration: The migration adds a new storage table but does not change existing metric names. Alert rule updates are additive and kept behind a feature flag `token_cost_delta_alerts`. Rolling migration plan in PR description for operators is included in the console doc link.

Operational considerations: Added a feature flag and a migration script (pkg/observability/storage/migrations/20260214_add_delta.sql) and a runbook entry for SREs in docs/runbooks/token-cost-delta.md describing how to revert tuning, inspect heatmaps, and run offline calibration.

Related: Linear ENG-5941 covers long-tail-noise reduction; OBS-112 tracks dashboard work. This PR touches both server and console and required cross-team review.

title: introduce-retry-bundles-and-stream-progress-telemetry-across-sdks
preview: Context: Customers have asked for a compact, declarative way to reuse retry semantics across SDKs and to receive lightweight progress telemetry for long-running streaming requests. This PR introduces (1) retry bundles — named retry/profile presets that can be referenced by "retryPolicy" on client config and (2) a stream-progress telemetry envelope emitted by the SDKs for long-running pull/push streams. Motivation: reduce duplicated retry configuration in apps and enable observability consumers (SLO dashboards, SRE) to surface stream progress without instrumenting app-level callbacks. Summary of changes: added a retry bundle registry and lookup time resolution in the client core; exposed a small opt-in "stream_progress" event channel that SDKs can emit at stable intervals (configurable); implemented consistent semantics across Python/TypeScript/Go; added unit and integration tests and bumped SDK versions. Checklist: - [x] Design doc: linear/ENG-3245 (linked) - [x] Cross-SDK API proposal reviewed by SDK leads - [x] Unit tests for registry lookup and fallback - [x] Integration tests on hosted-compat test harness - [x] Version bumps and changelogs updated - [x] E2E smoke in canary environment (observability) API compatibility: The client constructors accept an optional new field `retry_profile` where callers can pass either a string (bundle name) or an inline profile object. The default remains unchanged unless `retry_profile` is provided. The `stream_progress` channel is opt-in via `enable_stream_progress=true` in client config; when enabled the SDK emits JSON-serializable events that include a monotonic `offset_estimate`, `tokens_emitted`, and `elapsed_ms`. Commits summary: - Add retry-bundle registry, resolution and small DSL for preset values - Wire retry lookup into request pipeline and backoff scheduler - Add stream-progress envelope, interval runner, and config knobs in Py/TS/Go - Add unit tests and integration harness changes - Bump package versions and changelogs Changed files (high level): sdks/python/redwood/client.py, sdks/python/redwood/retry_bundles.py, sdks/python/tests/test_retry_bundles.py, sdks/typescript/src/client.ts, sdks/typescript/src/stream_progress.ts, sdks/typescript/test/retry-bundles.test.ts, sdks/go/client/retry_registry.go, sdks/go/stream/progress.go, docs/sdk/retry-profiles.md Testing notes: - Initial CI run failed lint in TS due to export ordering; fixed in commit 3. - Integration runs on the hosted-canary revealed a timing edge where stream-progress events could be too frequent for very high-throughput streams; added a min-interval clamp and made the interval adaptive. Backwards compatibility and migration: No breaking changes. Existing callers not opting into `retry_profile` or `enable_stream_progress` see no change. Security: retry bundles are client-only; no new server-side surface is exposed. Observability: telemetry envelope follows semantic conventions in docs/sdk/telemetry.md. Related work: touches ENG-3301 (streaming-observability) and SUP-1182 (customer support case requesting standard retry presets).

title: introduce-session-bridge-and-ergonomic-retry-wrappers-across-sdks
preview:
Context: Multiple teams asked for a small, embeddable bridge to unify session/token lifecycle across SDKs while making retries and streaming ergonomics consistent. This PR introduces a compact session-bridge interface implemented in Python/TS/Go, ergonomic per-language retry wrappers, and an opt-in compat mode for older SDK consumers. Motivation: reduce duplication of token-refresh logic across service clients, make stream resumptions default-ergonomic, and provide a clear upgrade path with minimal breaking surface.

What this changes: - New core interface 'SessionBridge' (language idiomatic wrappers) that centralizes lease/renew/hand-off semantics. - Per-language retry profiles: 'default', 'interactive', 'high-throughput' with consistent backoff math and jitter shapes. - Stream ergonomics: a small cursor API for resuming chunked streams and an automatic token-warmup hook for first request in a session. - Opt-in compatibility mode: SDKs honor legacy token callback signatures when 'compatMode=true'. - Tests + CI: cross-sdk interop tests and matrix updated to run a lightweight interop job.

Checklist: - [x] design doc, ENG-2141 - [x] types and user docs - [x] integration tests - [x] version bumps for all three SDKs - [x] telemetry for token refresh events (new event type)

Review summary (summary of the review thread): Liam: asked for clearer failure semantics on session handoff; asked to guarantee idempotent handoff. Action: added handoff_id and idempotency checks in bridge implementation. Priya: requested smaller public surface for Python session manager and suggested default retry names; actioned and reduced exported symbols. Mateo: noted a flaky test on CI (ts stream resume) — root cause was a timing race in test harness; I switched to deterministic mock time and re-ran CI.

CI: initial run had one flaky TS test fail; re-run completed successfully across all matrix entries (linux/py, linux/ts, linux/go, macos/py). Coverage: +0.4% on cross-sdk tests.

Migration notes: bump SDKs to the versions in release notes. Existing consumers who relied on the older token callback should enable compatMode=true and migrate to the new async-friendly token provider by the next minor. Docs: added migration guide and example helpers in examples/streaming.

Risks & mitigations: The change surfaces auth lifecycle in more places — to mitigate we kept compatMode and added a feature-flagged rollout in the console for dedicated/private customers. No breaking changes expected by default.

Merge: Squash-merged after approvals from Liam and Priya and green CI.

title: Refactor presubmit artifact pruner and introduce dependency stewarding with lightweight runner
preview: Motivation: Presubmit latency and noisy dependency churn have been two recurring developer pain points. This PR refactors the presubmit artifact pruner to be stage-aware, introduces a lightweight presubmit runner that does fast lint+type checks for most PRs, and adds a Dependency Steward component (opt-in) that surfaces suspected dev-dep rot and creates curated rotation PRs. Summary of changes: - Stage-aware artifact pruner: pruner now prunes only artifacts for long-running lanes and preserves fastpath caches for tagged fast-lane PRs. This reduces redundant cache eviction and warms fastpath caches more reliably. - Lightweight presubmit runner: a small Go runner that executes a minimal lint/type subset using cached toolchains. New fastpath lane runs in ~30s on average for changed JS/TS/Go packages. - Dependency Steward: watches package lockfiles, runs a lightweight compatibility probe (matrix of latest patch/minor versions) and opens an automated PR template with suggested updates and risk tags. This component is opt-in via a repo label (deps/auto). - CI wiring and release hooks: composer for presubmit lanes adjusted to avoid running full integration matrix on docs-only or trivial changes; release smoke now runs only after merge in release lanes. - Observability: added presubmit-duration and dep-steward-created metrics, and a small dashboard link. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for pruner and runner - [x] E2E smoke in the fastpath lane - [x] Integration test for DepSteward probe harness - [x] Docs updated (devtools/presubmit.md) - [x] Linear tickets linked Implementation notes: commits are structured to separate the pruner refactor from the new runner and the dep steward. The runner is intentionally minimal; any heavy checks are still gated into the existing heavy-lane workflows. Security: DepSteward does not have write-by-default; it opens PRs and requires repo-level approval. The agent uses the existing bot user and KMS keys. Migration plan: rollout is staged: (1) enable fastpath lane for a small set of repos, (2) monitor presubmit-duration and CI failure modes for two weeks, (3) enable DepSteward on opt-in repos and monitor for false positives. Related: ENG-2142 (presubmit performance), ENG-3384 (dep rotation pilot). Links: internal doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2142/presubmit-fastpath-runner and design spec in docs/devtools/dep-steward.md.

title: decouple-init-configmap-and-introduce-tenant-rotation-operator-helpers
preview: Motivation: In private installs we need a small, immutable init ConfigMap consumed by several init jobs and a lightweight tenant-rotation helper that can be turned on/off independently. This PR splits the monolithic init template into a minimal configmap, introduces a tenant-rotation helper template and CRD scaffold for use by on-prem operators, and tightens the values schema for preflight probes and secret mapping. Changes include a compatibility shim so existing installs are not broken; rollout plan below. title: Introduce compact-delta aggregator and span-primer sampling with contextual alert gating
preview: Context and motivation: We currently surface token-level cost and latency breakdowns and long-tail alerts, but we lack a compact, query-efficient signal that captures the delta between streamed/committed tokens and final generator decision sequences across invocations. This causes noisy alerts for streaming-heavy routes and makes it hard to prioritize log capture for expensive anomalies. This PR introduces two orthogonal improvements: (1) a compact-delta aggregator (stream-delta-meter) that computes a lightweight fingerprint and byte/ token delta histogram per invocation, suitable for rollups; and (2) a span-primer sampler that prefetches higher-fidelity logs and span context for invocations whose compact-delta signatures match configured entropy or pattern thresholds. On top of those we add a contextual alert gating policy (quiet_gate) that reduces alert churn by conditioning alerts on delta stability and recent run-rate of similar fingerprints. Summary of changes: - Introduce stream-delta-meter: a runtime-side metric that emits per-invocation compact hashes, token delta counts (prefetch vs commit), and a small entropy score. Aggregates are designed for rollup storage (low cardinality keys + reservoir histograms). - Add span-primer sampler: a lightweight pre-sampler integrated into the observability sidecar which, given a fingerprint and entropy, will pre-attach a small log buffer and request span enrichment only for candidates above a configurable threshold. The sampler is tunable per-tenant and per-route. - Add quiet_gate alert conditioning: a filter that subscribes to compact-delta rollups and SLO state; alerts are muted for short windows when matched fingerprints have stable low-entropy behavior, and re-enabled when variance increases. - Dashboard additions: new panels in observability/default-invocations.json showing compact-delta heatmap, delta-to-cost scatter, and primer hit-rate. - Tests and benchmarks: unit tests for aggregator, e2e system test with synthetic streaming traces, and a small perf benchmark to ensure meter adds <1% p99 overhead under 1000req/s on local bench. Design notes and rationale: - Compact deltas are intentionally lossy: we trade exact reconstruction for low-cardinality signal useful at scale. The hash + histogram allows joining to traces when we escalate only for interesting patterns. - Span-primer reduces log storage and rate of expensive span hydration by steering enrichment to likely-high-value invocations. - quiet_gate is a pragmatic middle-ground between strict suppression and noisy alerts; tuned conservatively by default. Checklist: - [x] runtime metric implementation and unit tests - [x] sidecar sampler + integration tests - [x] dashboard panels and example queries - [x] alert rule + default gating policy - [x] perf benchmark and basic throughput test Deployment plan: staged rollout by tenant: 1) feature off by default in main; 2) enable for internal telemetry-qa tenant and observe primer hit-rate; 3) ramp to canary for <10% of traffic on observability namespace with quiet_gate in monitoring-only mode; 4) flip gating to active after 48h stable canary. Rollback: feature flags at runtime for both stream-delta-meter and span-primer; quiet_gate can be disabled centrally. Backwards compatibility: metric schema adds a v2 tag; aggregators fall back to v1 path when v2 not present. Related work: this complements existing prefetch-warmup-metrics (pr-577890) but focuses on compact fingerprints and gating rather than prefetch efficiency. Also ties into token-cost-atlas efforts for downstream attribution. Review notes: reviewers asked for clear cardinality constraints and privacy review for hashing; hashing scheme uses HMAC with tenant key and is documented in SECURITY.md. Performance testing and CI: CI run passed (unit/integration/linters). Benchmarks included microbench script results in /observability/bench/compact_delta/bench.md. Post-merge action items: (1) add a telemetry dashboard to SLO-runbook linking primer hits to alert history, (2) schedule a short playbook with oncall to observe quiet_gate behavior for first week.

title: Introduce credential-gateway + pullstream-sugar: compact session routing & ergonomic async generators
preview:
Summary: This PR introduces a lightweight 'credential-gateway' component plus a set of 'pullstream-sugar' adapters that provide ergonomic async-generator/pullstream facades across the Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs. The goal is to centralize credential selection & prefetch logic (local + delegated chains) and to expose a small, consistent async iteration surface for streaming responses that reduces boilerplate in user code.

Motivation and context: Multiple teams reported friction wiring credential selection into streaming callers: duplicated prefetch logic, inconsistent failure semantics during mid-stream reauth, and divergent ergonomics across SDKs. Instead of further ad-hoc fixes in each SDK we built a thin orchestrator (credential-gateway) that: 1) mediates credential lookup and fallback policies, 2) exposes a compact lease/claim API for streams, and 3) exposes hooks used by language adapters. The pullstream-sugar adapters implement a small ergonomic wrapper (async generator in Python/TS, channel-like iterator in Go) that: - automatically primes credentials for a session, - surfaces a single typed StreamHandle with .close/.pause hints, and - reconciles retry/backoff signals from the runtime into a stable consumer contract.

What changed (high-level):
- Introduced internal/cred/gateway.go and internal/cred/gateway_pyshim.py: core routing and lease semantics.
- Added sdk/python/redwood/pullstream_sugar.py: ergonomic async generator + examples.
- Added sdk/ts/src/pullstreamSugar.ts: compact async iterable wrapper and TypeScript declarations.
- Added sdk/go/pullstream/sugar.go: channel-backed stream adapter and small helper API.
- Bumped SDK versions and package metadata: python 1.13.0, typescript 2.8.0, go v1.5.2.
- Tests: new unit tests for gateway routing matrix + integration harness exercising mid-stream credential rotation and a mock runtime.

title: staged-rollback-with-dependency-graph-and-state-seed-preservation
preview: Context: customers running Private deployments reported rare failures when performing multi-stage upgrades where component inter-dependencies (schema, config, seed data) required coordinated rollbacks. Motivation: provide a safe, testable staged rollback path that understands service dependency ordering and preserves tenant-visible seeded state so that partial rollbacks do not leave the cluster in an inconsistent or unbootable condition. What this PR does: 1) Introduces a reversible dependency graph used by the installer to compute rollback stages; edges cover DB migrations, config map velocity, helm chart ordering, and tenant seed/data artifacts. 2) Adds ‘seed-preserve’ mode: when enabled, pre-rollback hooks snapshot tenant-seeded objects (application-level seeds and lightweight KV entries) into encrypted, versioned manifests stored in object storage with a TTL and rotation policy. 3) Implements a staged rollback executor that runs rollback stages in reverse topological order with signal checkpoints so operators can pause between stages and run integration smoke tests. 4) Adds a cli subcommand “installer rollback-plan --dry-run” that emits a human-readable plan and an operator-safe JSON plan; the JSON includes per-stage healthchecks and required preconditions. 5) Adds automatic downgrade guard checks that prevent application binary downgrades if schema compatibility checks fail; these checks are pluggable to allow product teams to opt-in. 6) Adds telemetry events and RBAC-scoped audit logs for each rollback stage and seed snapshot operation. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear: ENG-2345) [x] unit tests for plan generator and serializer [x] integration test harness for staged executor using local Vagrant cluster [x] end-to-end dry-run validated against a sample two-tenant cluster [x] documented operational playbook and CLI usage (docs/installer/rollback.md) Testing notes: dry-run and encrypted snapshot creation were exercised against a local postgres instance and minio; tests include simulated partial failure (post-stage failure) and observed automatic resume/retry behavior. Compatibility: default behavior is opt-out; installer flags preserved for existing installs. Rollout plan: feature behind feature flag enabled by default for new private installs, and behind opt-in for existing customers; will be gated by a 2-week beta with selected customers. Rollback plan: staged executor supports resume-from-stage and manual abort; seed-preserve manifests can be used to restore tenant seeds if automated rollback cannot complete. Related work: complements earlier safe-rollback and migration-preview tooling. Security: snapshots are encrypted with customer-provided KMS keys when available; rotation TTL of 30 days by default. Limitations: not targeted at large binary data restores (those remain outside scope); heavy tenant-count clusters may need longer pause windows. Migration path: installer will validate cluster state and optionally recommend a canary rollback before full rollback. Links: design doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2345, sample plan output in PR files. Commit summary: a) add dependency-graph package and graph serialization; b) implement plan generator and JSON emitter; c) implement staged executor with pause/resume; d) seed snapshotter and encryption layer; e) CLI flags and documentation; f) integration tests and telemetry hooks; g) unit tests and mocks. Files changed (high level): installer/cmd/rollback.go; installer/pkg/depgraph/* (new); installer/pkg/seedshot/* (new); installer/pkg/executor/staged.go; docs/installer/rollback.md; charts/redwood/values.rollback.yaml; scripts/e2e/rollback-smoke.sh; proto/installer/audit.proto; Makefile updates. Review conversation summary: initial review requested a smaller default TTL and clearer UI for pause/resume. Connor: "Please make TTL configurable and ensure KMS fallback behavior is explicit in logs." Author: "Made TTL configurable via --snapshot-ttl and added KMS fallback log lines; tests updated." Mateo: "Executor should surface stage-level metrics for SLOs." Author: "Added basic counters and trace spans; will iterate on dashboards in a follow-up." Sana: "Does this change the default behavior for existing installs?" Author: "No, default opt-out for upgrades; new installs get opt-in true but flagged in release notes." CI: pipeline ran unit, lint, and integration jobs; one flaky e2e job failed initially and was re-run; final CI green. Merge outcome: squash-merged to main after approvals and green CI. Release notes: "Added staged rollback support with dependency-aware execution and optional seed-state preservation. New CLI tools: installer rollback-plan and installer rollback --resume. Snapshots are encrypted with KMS when configured. Opt-in for existing clusters."

title: Introduce steady-state burst-window controller and utilization breathing reporter
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see oscillatory behavior in Dedicated fleet autoscaling when traffic presents high-frequency microbursts layered on slow baseline drift. Existing heuristics conflate transient spikes with sustained load, which causes either wasted warm capacity or repeated scale reactions. This PR introduces two complementary pieces: a burst-window controller (steady-state-friendly) and a utilization 'breathing' reporter that surfaces transient/sustained composition in telemetry.

Design summary: The burst-window controller allocates ephemeral burst windows (short-duration capacity allowances) separate from long-lived reservation commitments. Windows are computed per-tenant and per-pool. A short window (t_short default 30s) permits handling microbursts with minimal reservation changes; a sustained window (t_long default 5m) evaluates whether to escalate burst allowance into a reservation commit or to drain the burst. The utilization breathing reporter emits a normalized breathing_index gauge and bucketed histograms to differentiate sudden spikes from baseline increases. Breathing index = (p95 - p50) / (p50 + eps), smoothed by EWMA with configurable alpha. We also emit short and long window occupancy counters and a headroom_utilization_ratio to help autoscaler tuning.

Implementation notes: Changes touch the autoscaler and telemetry subsystems:
- autoscaler/controller/burst_window_controller.go: new controller orchestrating ephemeral windows and escalation rules. Implements soft credits and leaky replenishment.
- autoscaler/ledger/reservation_api.go: lightweight hooks to create ephemeral burst reservations anchored to short windows.
- telemetry/reporters/breathing_reporter.go: computes rolling quantiles using tdigest + time-decayed buckets; emits: breathing_index, burst_window_open_ratio, sustained_commit_rate.
- runtime/pool/metrics_tags.go: adds tenant/pool tags needed for per-tenant breathing metrics and rate-limited emission.
- e2e/autoscale_integration_test.go: new integration tests that replay traces with layered microbursts and validate controller behavior.

Algorithmic details (brief):
- Quantile windows: use sliding-window tdigest reservoirs for short (30s) and long (5m) windows.
- Breathing index smoothing: breathing_smoothed[t] = alpha * breathing_raw[t] + (1-alpha) * breathing_smoothed[t-1]. Default alpha=0.2.
- Escalation rule: if short_window_util > short_threshold && long_window_util > escalate_threshold && sustained_commit_budget_available => convert ephemeral burst to reservation commit. Otherwise, allow burst to expire and charge soft credits.
- Soft credits: tenants receive a capped replenishment rate when under the short window; credits decay over time to prevent long-term overcommit.

Testing and results: Local bench and CI integration replay using synthetic traces show a 38% reduction in unnecessary reservation churn (measured as number of reservation commits rolled back within 10m) and a 12% improvement in 99th latency under noisy burst workloads. Telemetry visualization in staging reproduces distinct breathing_index spikes aligned to transient traffic bursts.

Rollout plan:
1) Canary rollout to 10% of Dedicated fleet in staging pools for 48h with detailed telemetry retention.
2) Observe breathing_index, commit rate, and reservation churn.
3) Gradual ramp to 50% then 100% if metrics are stable.

Checklist:
- [x] Controller implemented with config knobs
- [x] Telemetry reporter added and gated by feature flag (default on for staging)
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Docs: brief explainer added to internal autoscaler operational runbook (linked below)

Related: ENG-2319 (stability improvements), SUP-4021 (customer incident that motivated analysis).

title: unify-ci-actions-introduce-release-automation-and-cleanup-dev-deps
preview:
Motivation: our GitHub Actions setup had grown organic cruft: duplicated workflow YAML, inconsistent caching, and ad-hoc release steps across repos. This PR consolidates reusable workflows, standardizes caches and test matrix behavior, and enables deterministic release automation using release-please. The change set is focused on developer-experience and repo hygiene (no product logic changes).

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds .github/workflows/reusable-ci.yml and reusable-lint.yml to centralize build/test/lint behavior.
- Replaces three repo-specific workflows (ci-python.yml, ci-node.yml, ci-go.yml) with small entrypoint workflows that invoke the reusable workflows.
- Introduces a common action cache policy (key includes os, runner, node-version/python-version, and lockfile hash) to reduce cold-cache flakiness.
- Adds a test sharding strategy using matrix.include shards for the python test suite and parallelization hints for pytest-xdist when applicable.
- Migrates dependency automation from dependabot to a unified renovate configuration with onboarding for major monorepo rules (lockfile maintenance, group upgrades for devDeps).
- Bumps key CI dev deps: actions/checkout@v4, actions/cache@v4, pnpm/setup-pnpm@v2, actions/setup-node@v4 and setup-python@v4.
- Adds a release-please config and a GitHub workflow (release-please.yml) to create draft releases and CHANGELOG entries using conventional commits. This will replace manual release steps and the previous ad-hoc scripts in scripts/release/*.
- Removes legacy shell-based lint/test wrappers in scripts/ci/ in favor of reusable composite actions under .github/actions/ci-helpers/.
- Small refactors to tests to make them hermetic: pinning ephemeral network calls in two integration tests and reducing reliance on system time via freezegun in python tests.

Checklist (manual steps validated):
- [x] Cross-repo reusable workflow shape validated locally with act and against a staging branch in a sandbox repo.
- [x] Cache keys stress-tested (cold/warm) across linux/macos runners.
- [x] release-please created draft release during a dry-run using --dry-run.
- [x] Dependabot->Renovate migration: tested PR creation on a small sample group repository.
- [x] Flaky tests observed in previous builds were stabilized (see review comments).

How to review: the core is the reusable workflows and the release config; I recommend reviewing the YAML files first (.github/workflows and .github/actions), then the scripts/ci removal. Test-specific changes are intentionally small and annotated inline.

Rollout plan: merge to main will flip on the reusable workflows by renaming the old workflows to .disabled (kept for a week for quick rollback). After two successful main runs we will delete the .disabled files and mark this PR as fully applied. Release automation will be enabled in day-1 but set to create draft releases only (no auto-publish) until we validate changelog generation for 2 release cycles.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: subtle differences in runner env captured by old per-repo workflows. Mitigation: included explicit runner labels and expanded matrix combinations in reusable workflow; staged dry runs were executed.
- Risk: renovate onboarding churn. Mitigation: initial schedule is weekly and grouping policy limits PR noise for devDeps.

Related work items: ENG-4021, ENG-4278, OPS-189.

Notes: this PR intentionally keeps functional changes out of scope; any follow-up feature changes should be in separate PRs.

- Adds a test sharding strategy using matrix.include shards for the python test suite and parallelization hints for pytest-xdist when applicable.
- Migrates dependency automation from dependabot to a unified renovate configuration with onboarding for major monorepo rules (lockfile maintenance, group upgrades for devDeps).
- Bumps key CI dev deps: actions/checkout@v4, actions/cache@v4, pnpm/setup-pnpm@v2, actions/setup-node@v4 and setup-python@v4.
- Adds a release-please config and a GitHub workflow (release-please.yml) to create draft releases and CHANGELOG entries using conventional commits. This will replace manual release steps and the previous ad-hoc scripts in scripts/release/*.
- Removes legacy shell-based lint/test wrappers in scripts/ci/ in favor of reusable composite actions under .github/actions/ci-helpers/.
- Small refactors to tests to make them hermetic: pinning ephemeral network calls in two integration tests and reducing reliance on system time via freezegun in python tests.

Checklist (manual steps validated):
- [x] Cross-repo reusable workflow shape validated locally with act and against a staging branch in a sandbox repo.
- [x] Cache keys stress-tested (cold/warm) across linux/macos runners.
- [x] release-please created draft release during a dry-run using --dry-run.
- [x] Dependabot->Renovate migration: tested PR creation on a small sample group repository.
- [x] Flaky tests observed in previous builds were stabilized (see review comments).

How to review: the core is the reusable workflows and the release config; I recommend reviewing the YAML files first (.github/workflows and .github/actions), then the scripts/ci removal. Test-specific changes are intentionally small and annotated inline.

Rollout plan: merge to main will flip on the reusable workflows by renaming the old workflows to .disabled (kept for a week for quick rollback). After two successful main runs we will delete the .disabled files and mark this PR as fully applied. Release automation will be enabled in day-1 but set to create draft releases only (no auto-publish) until we validate changelog generation for 2 release cycles.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: subtle differences in runner env captured by old per-repo workflows. Mitigation: included explicit runner labels and expanded matrix combinations in reusable workflow; staged dry runs were executed.
- Risk: renovate onboarding churn. Mitigation: initial schedule is weekly and grouping policy limits PR noise for devDeps.

Related work items: ENG-4021, ENG-4278, OPS-189.

Notes: this PR intentionally keeps functional changes out of scope; any follow-up feature changes should be in separate PRs.

title: Introduce rate-limiter smoothing, egress soft-deny probe, and regional telemetry guard
preview: Motivation: During an intermittent incident on 2026-02-12 we observed rapid oscillation between normal and soft-deny states in regional egress when transient backend latency spikes coincided with bursty tenant traffic. That interaction exposed two issues: (1) the token-based rate limiter emits sharp step changes that produce aggressive load-shedding decisions, and (2) the regional health probe lacked hysteresis causing frequent region failover toggles. This PR introduces three coordinated mitigations: a lightweight smoothing layer for rate limits (EMWA-based), an 'egress soft-deny probe' that allows a short probationary holding pattern before full soft-deny enforcement, and a telemetry guard that prevents automated failovers when we detect probe/metric oscillation patterns. Summary of changes: - runtime/shedder/smoother.go: new smoothing wrapper around the existing leaky-bucket limiter using an exponential moving weighted average (configurable alpha) to soften ramp-up/ramp-down. - runtime/egress/soft_deny_probe.go: probationary probe that tracks transient rejection rates and only flips soft-deny on sustained windows; supports configurable window and grace counters. - telemetry/region_guard.go: guard that computes oscillation score from region-probe and egress metrics and inhibits automated regional failover if score exceeds threshold; emits guard telemetry for SRE dashboards. - k8s/patches/egress-configmap.yaml: runtime defaults and feature flag toggles to stage rollout. - tests/integration/egress_smoothing_test.go: new integration and deterministic fuzz tests that simulate bursty tenants + backend hitches. - docs/ops/egress-smoothing.md: runbook, rollout, and rollback steps. Backwards compatibility: all features are behind feature flags and conservative defaults (alpha=0.25, probation window=30s, oscillation threshold high). Rollout plan: canary 25% (us-west-2) for 2 hours, then region-by-region rollout with a 2-hour observation window and a quick rollback playbook. Hotfix rationale: this was prioritized as an incident-driven hotfix (SUP-4523) because oscillation previously caused two small regional failovers within a 45m window. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for smoothing math and boundary conditions - [x] Integration test that reproduces previously-observed oscillation in CI fuzz harness - [x] Updated runbook and alert thresholds - [x] Telemetry events wired and dashboard updated - [x] Feature flag gating for progressive rollout Deploy notes: flip feature flag to canary, observe metrics: region.failover.count, egress.soft_deny.rate, shedder.smoothing.alpha; if regional oscillation returns, revert flag and tag incident for deeper root cause. Related: ENG-7891 (rate-limiter stability), SRE-141 (egress incident postmortem).

title: Add offline Helm OCI seeding and precompiled BPF-CNI module bundles for air-gapped kernels
preview: Motivation: Customers running Redwood Private in fully air-gapped datacenters reported two recurring issues: 1) inconsistent CNI installs when kernels differ from our build matrix, and 2) manual, error-prone steps to seed private OCI/Helm registries. This PR introduces an end-to-end offline-friendly flow: packaged, precompiled BPF-CNI kernel module bundles for common enterprise kernels plus a new OCI-Helm seeder sidecar utility that can be run from the airgap bootstrap container to atomically seed registries and charts. Summary of changes: packaged kernel module artifacts per distro (ubuntu20/22, rhel8/9, rocky, almalinux) with vmlinuz/headers checksums; an installer hook that probes target nodes and selects the closest-compatible module bundle; an offline Helm OCI seeder that pulls from provided artifact tarballs and imports images/charts into the local registry with optional signature verification; terraform module updates to create a registry seed job and a registry credential secret; Helm chart tweaks to surface device-mapper and module-loader knobs; and tests and preflight checks added to the installer preflight. Design notes: To avoid kernel compilation in customer environments we ship prebuilt, signed .ko bundles plus a fingerprint manifest. The installer probes uname -r and glibc/kernel headers and performs a checksum-based compatibility match; if no exact match exists, it falls back to a closest ABI-compatible bundle and inserts a graceful warning in the console output and audit log. The OCI seeder uses an idempotent import layer: tarball->verify->import->promote so retries are safe. Security: all bundles and helm charts are signed with our airgap signing key and verified locally before install; signature failure aborts the seeding step and surfaces a clear remediation path. Migration and rollout: added a canary flag in the installer config (installer.canary.offline_module_loader=true) to enable gradual rollout on Dedicated/Private installs. For major customer migrations we provide a helper script that collects /lib/modules and system info to help produce a custom bundle. Testing: added unit tests for the module selector logic, integration test harness that runs the seeder against a local registry in CI (recorded in ci/seeder-e2e), and manual validation on Ubuntu 20.04 kernel variants. CI run notes: initial CI run failed linter on variable naming in terraform module; fixed in second commit and all checks passed. Backwards compatibility: the default remains the previous build-from-source flow unless the canary flag is set. Documentation: added docs/private/offline-seeding.md and a troubleshooting section for kernel mismatches. Rollout plan: merge to main, cut a 0.31.0-alpha private-installer image with seeder sidecar, enable canary for internal staging clusters, gather telemetry on module fallback events before broad rollout. Related: ENG-12458 tracks broader vendor bundle matrix expansion.
