title: human-latchable-canary-with-replayable-traffic-snapshots
preview:
Context: Some production rollouts require a human review gate when model changes touch high-risk paths (financial scoring, content filtering, PII transforms). Automated canaries and metric gates are necessary but not always sufficient when subtle distributional or failure-mode regressions appear. This PR implements a human-latchable canary flow that captures a replayable, compact snapshot of the traffic slice used for the canary, surfaces an inspection UI in the Console, and provides explicit accept/reject actions that can be recorded as part of the rollout decision. Motivation: give on-call/product owners a reproducible artifact (traffic snapshot + metadata + lightweight deterministic replay) so they can inspect model outputs offline, reproduce failure cases locally, and make a defensible, auditable manual decision without rolling back or keeping traffic frozen indefinitely. Goals: 1) Record a deterministic, privacy-aware snapshot of the canary traffic window (inputs, non-sensitive metadata, model response hashes, provenance) 2) Provide a fast UI for sampling and triage with redaction rules applied 3) Allow an operator to latch the canary into pause state and either approve (promote) or reject (auto-rollback) with a recorded rationale 4) Make snapshots small and signed so they can be archived and replayed locally with the same runtime pipeline and model variant 5) Integrate with existing rollout policies so the human latch is optional and composable with automated gates

What changed (high level): added a LatchController to the orchestrator runtime that can transition a canary to a PAUSED state and trigger a snapshot capture; implemented a replay_store component that packs sampled requests into a compact gzip+jsonl archive with signed manifest and anonymization hooks; extended the Console with a CanaryInspectPanel that lists samples, shows response diffs and allows downloading the replay package; added an API surface (openapi/rollouts.yaml) for latch actions and snapshot retrieval; updated CI pipeline to run the new integration test; added a runbook documenting when and how to use the human latch; small Terraform + Helm changes to surface the config toggle and RBAC for operators. The snapshot format is intentionally minimal: input tokens (truncated), deterministic response hash, request metadata (route, model_id, timestamp), and optional attached logs. Sensitive attributes are redacted by default via the anonymizer pipeline and the redaction rules are configurable per-customer.

Checklist:
- [x] LatchController implementation with state transitions and audit events
- [x] Snapshot packing + signing in replay_store
- [x] Console UI for triage + download
- [x] API contract and OpenAPI update
- [x] Integration tests covering pause, snapshot, replay download
- [x] Runbook and helm/terraform config
- [x] E2E CI job updated to include canary human-latch flow

High-level changed areas (files): services/orchestrator/src/canary (controller + replay store), console components for inspection, docs/runbooks, helm/values and RBAC, CI pipeline.

Testing and CI: local unit tests added for LatchController transitions and replay packing; added an integration test that spins up a lightweight orchestrator + dummy model to exercise capture and replay download (ci/pipeline was updated to run this in the canary stage). CI initially failed on lint (missing docstring in replay_store) and a flaky test; both were fixed in follow-ups. The overall pipeline now passes (see ci/pipeline/canary_pipeline.yml) and the PR shows green checks for build/test/lint/security-scan.

Backward compatibility and migration: The latch feature is gated behind a config flag (latch.enabled=false by default). Existing rollouts are unaffected unless operators opt-in. The snapshot schema is versioned (v1) and we included a lightweight backward-compatibility shim in replay_store to ignore unknown fields during replay. No DB migrations required; replay_store uses object storage buckets (S3-compatible) and stores manifests in a separate prefix.

Security and privacy: The anonymizer runs before snapshot packing and redacts fields matching configured patterns (PII, auth headers). Snapshot manifests are signed with the cluster key to prevent tampering. Access to snapshot download is RBAC-protected in Console and enforced at the API layer. We added a security-scan CI check to ensure no raw PII is persisted by default.

Operational notes: default behavior is to auto-archive snapshots for 7 days; retention can be configured per-tenant. Snapshots are intentionally limited to 5000 records and 5MB compressed to avoid storage blowup. The runbook documents emergency bypass procedures and how to run the local replay harness (scripts/replay/pack_snapshot.py + small container image used by SRE).

Open questions and future work: - Add per-metric sampling policies (sample biased by error class) - Integrate snapshot diffs into the Optimize pipeline for automated regression clustering - Add server-side replay runner to reproduce heavy tails in production without local env setup

Merge notes: squash-merged after approvals; migration not required; feature gated by config; rollout to production disabled by default. See release_notes below for operator-facing summary.

title: Telemetric cohort swing and rollback automaton for safe model promotes
preview:
Summary: Introduces the Rollback Automaton — a controller that coordinates telemetric cohort swings during promotions and performs automated, paced rollbacks when aggregated telemetry indicates SLO regressions. Motivation: existing canary flows rely on manual thresholds or simple gates that don't reason about temporal signal shape, noise, or correlated cohort regressions. This PR implements a telemetry-first adjudicator that reduces false positives and speeds safe rollbacks while preserving user experience. Design highlights: 1) Windowed signal aggregation: the automaton computes weighted rolling windows (short/medium/long) per cohort for latency, error-rate, and quality deltas; 2) Multi-metric decision graph: configurable decision nodes combine metrics with tunable weights and override rules (safety gates, urgent rollback paths); 3) Pacing and swing plan: when a rollback is required, the controller performs a gradual cohort swing (e.g., 60%->40%->20% over N steps) with health checks between steps; 4) Hysteresis & cooldown: the controller enforces cooldowns and backoff to avoid oscillation; 5) Simulation mode and dry-run: operators can simulate automaton decisions against historical telemetry or replayed traffic. What changed:
- controller/rollback_automaton.go: new reconcile loop and core adjudicator logic.
- pkg/telemetry/aggregator.go: windowed aggregators, decaying weights, and data retention hooks.
- pkg/observability/metrics.yaml: new Prometheus metrics (rollback_automaton_decision_total, cohort_window_latency_p50/p90, rollback_step_duration_seconds).
- controllers/e2e/telemetry_cohort_test.go: deterministic integration tests using the perf-canary harness with synthetic noise injection.
- docs/runbooks/rollback-automaton.md: runbook and tuning guide.
- configs/canary/pacing_defaults.yaml: default pacing profiles.
- scripts/replay/synthetic-replay.py: helper to simulate cohort traffic for local testing.
Commits summary: "init: automaton scaffolding and proto types"; "telemetry: windowed aggregator and retention"; "controller: decision graph + pacing engine"; "tests: e2e harness and unit coverage"; "ci: fix lint, add test fixture serialize"; "docs: rollout runbook and examples".
Testing & safety: unit tests cover decision nodes and window math; integration tests run the automaton against the perf-canary harness with 10k-sample replays exercising edge cases (burst error spikes, gradual drift, transient N+1 noise). We added a simulation mode that writes decisions to a dry-run log and never mutates traffic. Rollout plan: merge to main behind a feature flag (controllers.rollback_automaton.enabled=false). Initial rollout will be opt-in: we will enable automaton for internal staging only for 72 hours, run canary-replay scenarios and shadow-mode experiments for A/B lanes. After verifying stability, enable in production for low-traffic cohorts with a conservative pacing profile. Observability: new Grafana dashboard panel shipped with metrics.yaml, alert rules suggested in docs/runbooks/rollback-automaton.md. Operators should watch rollback_automaton_decision_total{outcome!="noop"} and cohort_window_latency_p90 spikes. Performance: introduced micro-optimizations to aggregators to keep CPU overhead sub-1% on control-plane nodes at 10k cohorts; bench harness shows <8ms median reconcile latency for typical cohort sizes. Backwards compatibility: controller is opt-in; existing canary and promote APIs unchanged. Migration: no data migration required. Known limitations: - does not yet ingest external quality signals (ML eval scores) — extension planned; - the decision graph reduces to simple gated rollback if telemetry is sparse. Related work: builds on ideas from ENG-1623 (staged-canary-scheduler) and complements the deferred-shadow-buffer PR; aligns with PM-468 rollout playbooks. Changelog: operators can tune weights via configs/canary/pacing_defaults.yaml. Security: no new network surface exposed. KMS/HSM unaffected. Runbook: see docs/runbooks/rollback-automaton.md for actionable steps to triage a false-positive rollback. Merge: squash and merge after approvals and CI green and docs update.

title: Introduce interleaved synthetic probe scheduler for hybrid-traffic canaries
preview: Motivation: On high-variance production traffic some canary detectors produce noisy signals because synthetic probes either flood the system or are too sparse to catch transient regressions. This PR implements an interleaved synthetic probe scheduler that mixes lightweight background probes with targeted burst probes keyed to observed traffic phases, reducing probe-induced noise while preserving detection sensitivity. Summary of changes: 1) New scheduler module perf-canary/scheduler/interleaved_scheduler.py implementing a two-tier schedule: low-rate continuous probes and phase-triggered burst probes; smart jitter and backoff to avoid synchronized bursts across instances; probe prioritization by route SLO weight. 2) Autotune logic in perf-canary/autotune/phase_detector.py that infers phase boundaries from short-term throughput and concurrency fingerprints and adjusts burst window size. 3) Canary evaluation adapter perf-canary/evaluator/interleaved_adapter.py that consumes mixed probe streams and feeds an updated detector that weights probe samples differently depending on probe class. 4) Small change to the traffic generator used in canary lab perf-canary/tests/traffic_fixtures.py to support hybrid traffic shaping and deterministic replay for unit tests. 5) New integration tests perf-canary/tests/test_interleaved_scheduler.py that simulate common phase-shift scenarios and assert reduced false-positive rate and comparable detection latency. 6) Docs update docs/perf/canary-scheduler.md with configuration knobs and recommended defaults. Why this approach: interleaving keeps a baseline signal for long-tail regressions while burst probes provide concentrated sensitivity during suspected phase transitions. The autotune limits operator overhead and the scheduler includes a global-rate cap to prevent probe stomping during incidents. Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind perf.canary.interleaved_scheduler, default off for all clusters. Canary on a single canary-controller pool for two weeks, measure false-positive rate, detection latency, and probe-cost uplift. If metrics are within thresholds we will enable for Stage and then gradually to Prod, with an immediate opt-out via runtime flag. Checklist: - [x] design doc reviewed by SRE and Applied ML - [x] unit and integration tests added - [x] performance microbenchmarks (see bench/README.md) - [x] docs updated - [x] feature flag gating in config - [x] linear tickets created for rollout and dashboards Details about detection model changes: the evaluator now applies a hierarchical sample weight: baseline probes are downweighted for short-window detectors to avoid washout, burst probes have higher weight for change-point detectors; combined score computes posterior using a small Bayesian smoothing term to avoid single-probe amplification. Impact on metrics: expected probe token uplift ~0.8% at default settings, probe CPU additional 0.3% on canary controllers during bursts; false positive rate reduced by ~35% in our synthetic phase-shift tests while median detection latency remained within 5-10% of baseline. Backwards compatibility: existing detectors continue to run unchanged unless the feature flag is enabled. Migration notes and config knobs are documented. Related work: builds on ENG-2145 phase fingerprint work and complements recent anomaly fusion changes in perf-canary. Risks and mitigations: risk of probe cascades across controllers mitigated by the global-rate cap and randomized jitter; risk of overfitting phase detection mitigated by conservative default knobs and a training cooldown window. Monitoring and dashboards: added canary_scheduler.{probe_rate,burst_rate,burst_count,phase_inference_confidence} metrics and dashboard templates in dashboards/canary-scheduler.json. Rollback safety: runtime flag immediate disable, and abort-on-anomaly if false-positive rate exceeds threshold for 1h. Merge outcome and rationale: after addressing initial review comments around concurrency in the scheduler and adding a safety cap to the burst backoff, the PR passed CI and was squash-merged into main to simplify revert.

title: openai-v1: synthesize-logprobs-fallback + stream-boundary-synchronizer for fragmented tools
preview:
Motivation: Some upstream open checkpoint variants and third-party model binaries sometimes omit token-level logprobs or produce incomplete logprob payloads when streaming tool/function outputs. Client integrations (and the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compat layer) expect consistent presence or a clearly defined fallback behavior; otherwise SDKs and streaming consumers must special-case many rare models. Separately, fragmented tool outputs (tool responses emitted in multiple partial stream frames) caused out-of-order 'end-of-tool' markers in a small number of production traces, leading to truncated tool results for function-calling flows.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a configurable logprobs_fallback_policy to the OpenAI compatibility adapter that supports three strategies: "prefer_model" (use model-provided logprobs if present), "synthesize" (estimate per-token logprobs using a light-weight softmax approximation over top-k logits when the model omits them), and "omit" (leave logprobs absent and mark the message metadata). Default remains "prefer_model" to preserve historical surface. 2) Implements a stream-boundary-synchronizer in the runtime streaming coordinator that buffers and sequences tool/function fragments until a definite terminal token or explicit tool_complete sentinel is observed, then emits deterministic framed events to the client. The synchronizer also exposes heuristics for max-fragment-linger and early-flush to avoid unbounded buffering. 3) Normalizes stop param variants from OpenAI-form inputs (string | array | null) into a canonical array at the compatibility layer and ensures stop tokens are honored consistently across synthesized logprob paths. 4) Adds a diagnostic flag `x-rw-debug-stream-fragments` to trace fragment reassembly for egress logs without leaking PII.

Compatibility notes: This change keeps the external /v1/chat.completions surface unchanged; new behavior is controlled by an adapter-level config flag and a small optional parameter (`logprobs_fallback_policy`) in the wire for advanced customers. Synthesis uses only top-k logits (k configurable, default 16) and is disabled for models that do not expose logits at all.

Testing and rollout: Unit tests cover param coercion permutations, synthesized logprob consistency checks, and stream fragment ordering. Integration tests run a small simulated runner that emits fragmented tool payloads. Feature gated behind a runtime flag and released with a canary rollout to 5% of hosted traffic before full rollout.

Related work: ENG-1934 (stream fragment coordinator), ENG-2110 (openai compat param normalization), SUP-5271 (support ticket reproducing missing logprobs).

title: installer: tenant-zone CMK placement + installer-signed-audit-envelopes
preview: Background: customers running private/on-prem deployments requested stronger guarantees that customer-managed keys (CMKs) are provisioned and used only within their configured data zone and that audit events emitted by the installer are tamper-evident for compliance audits. This PR adds installer-side enforcement of CMK placement (zone mapping) and an optional installer audit-envelope signer that produces a signed, immutable wrapper for audit batches so customers can retain a verifiable trail outside the control plane. Scope: - Preflight and onboarding validation for tenant->zone CMK mapping (installer preflight fails if CMK references a different region/zone) - Terraform + Helm changes to surface a tenant_kms_map var that binds tenant identifiers to allowed KMS zones - Installer KMS affinity controller that ensures API calls for key usage are routed only to allowed endpoints and rejects cross-zone CMK refs - An installer audit-proxy that signs audit batches with a tenant-scoped signing key (optionally backed by the customer's KMS) and writes signed envelopes to the configured audit sink - Migration helper: attempts safe rebind when an existing CMK is in an unexpected zone and emits a migration plan for operators - Feature flag: infra.install.kms_zone_enforce (default ON for new installs; existing installs are opt-in until migration is applied) Checklist: - [x] Preflight failing behavior implemented and unit-tested - [x] Terraform var + docs added - [x] Helm templates and chart values updated - [x] Audit envelope signer implemented with backward-compatible toggle - [x] Migration tool and a dry-run mode - [x] End-to-end smoke that runs in the CI private-installer job - [x] Security review (SEC-88) completed Design notes and motivation: preventing accidental cross-zone CMK usage reduces blast radius for data residency policies and is required by some customers' contractual controls. The signed audit envelopes provide an append-only proof for regulatory auditors without requiring the control plane to be fully trusted; operators can export these signed envelopes to their long-term retention stores. Implementation details: - installer/bin/preflight-check: new checks validate kms mapping and fail fast with suggested remediation commands - installer/terraform/kms_binding.tf: adds tenant_kms_map variable and validation rules; plan shows a migration recommendation if mismatched - helm/templates/kms-binding.yaml: renders Kubernetes secrets/configmaps that bind installer components to the allowed KMS endpoints - installer/cmd/kms-affinity-controller: light-weight controller that enforces runtime usage of mapped endpoints; rejects attempts to use unbound CMKs (returns 403 to caller) - installer/cmd/audit-signer: signs JSON-lines audit batches using PKCS#7 envelopes; verification helper included in installer/bin/verify-audit-envelope - charts/private-installer/values.schema.json: schema bump; new top-level keys under kms and audit documented - tests/ci/kms-affinity-smoke.sh and tests/unit/test_audit_signer.py added - docs/installer/kms-region-pin.md: operator facing doc and migration steps Rollout plan: - Canary: enable feature for a single internal tenant group in staging for 48h - Migration: run migration-tool dry-run across existing installs, notify operators of required action, then apply opt-in flag to migrate - Broad rollout: enable enforcement for new installs only for two weeks, then begin outreach to existing customers with an auto-migration path and operator-run command. Backout: toggling infra.install.kms_zone_enforce=false disables enforcement immediately; audit-signer can be disabled independently. Security considerations: the installer stores only a reference to the tenant signing key by default; signing may be delegated to customer CMKs via the KMS plugin. Access to signing keys is guarded by RBAC and the installer will log all signing operations as part of the audit envelope. Testing: unit tests added for preflight, controller rejection cases, and signer verification. CI: initial run flagged a flaky test that was hardened; final CI passed. Docs: new operator guide and migration playbook. Related: ENG-4012 (installer security hardening), SEC-88 (audit-envelope design).

title: add-invocation-telemetry-deduper-and-token-collapse-visualizer
preview: Introduce a lightweight Telemetry Deduper service and a Token Collapse Visualizer dashboard to reduce noisy duplicate spans and surface redundant token-level cost patterns. Motivation: during several incidents and routine SLO tuning we observed high cardinality and alert fatigue caused by duplicated spans emitted from the serving runtime (frontend retries, duplicate instrumentation points, and batched replays). This PR implements a deterministic fingerprinting pipeline that collapses semantically-identical spans early in the observability ingestion path and a dashboard that helps engineers understand when token costs are dominated by collapsed/duplicate traffic. The change includes a TTL-backed fingerprint cache, session-aware collapse heuristics, an orthogonal latency decomposition helper ("orthogonalizer") that attributes latency to queue/io/model phases for collapsed invocations, alert tuning rules to avoid duplicate alert storms, end-to-end tests with synthetic replay, and a Grafana dashboard with an interactive token-collapse Sankey and cost-savings calculator. Backwards compatibility: original spans are still retained in sampled snapshots; deduped counts are emitted as both raw_count and deduped_count so downstream consumers can opt-in. This description includes: design rationale, implementation summary, QA notes, rollout plan, and linked Linear/Jira items.

preview: Adds an internal release note entry for runtime 1.22 covering KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction, and links directly to the Confluence design doc + operational runbook so oncall/SRE have a single place to start during rollout. This is intentionally light on implementation detail (already covered in the design doc and PR series) and focuses on: default behavior by tier, rollout guardrails/kill-switch, and what to monitor (fragmentation ratio, bytes moved, eviction rate, and correlation with p99). title: Lightweight quality spot-check hooks for perf suite runs
preview:
Summary: Perf regressions are easy to detect, but we’ve had a few near-misses where perf-focused changes accidentally altered decoding behavior (e.g., defaults drift, chat template mismatch, tool-call formatting toggled, or truncation due to max_tokens changes). This PR adds an optional, lightweight “quality spot-check” hook to run at the end of a perf run, sampling a small prompt set and applying basic sanity checks. It is not intended to be a full quality gate; it is intended to catch obvious breakages early and provide artifacts for triage.

Motivation / context: As part of the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” work, we’re standardizing perf runs and integrating canaries/gating. Applied ML asked for minimal guardrails so that we don’t ship a perf win that is actually a decoding/config regression. This PR implements the minimal spot-check plan aligned with the internal memo “perf-vs-quality-guardrails-q1-2026” and is designed to be safe to run in CI and on benchmark fleets.

What’s included: (1) A new optional spot-check phase: `eval-harness spotcheck` can be invoked standalone, or via the existing perf runner hook (`--quality-spotcheck`). The spot-check runs after perf metrics collection by default to avoid perturbing latency/throughput measurements. (2) Prompt set + sampling: Adds `prompts/spotcheck/v1/` with a small, synthetic + hand-curated “must-not-break” set (chat + completion + tool-call shaped prompts). Sampling is deterministic based on `--spotcheck-seed` (default ties to the perf run seed if present). No production prompt content is included. (3) Sanity checks (fast heuristics): non-empty output; UTF-8 decodes; no immediate server-side error indicators; maximum repetition guard (token-level repetition ratio threshold) to catch “stuck loop” regressions; finish_reason presence for supported backends; optional JSON parse check when the prompt declares `expect_json=true`; optional tool-call structure check when the prompt declares `expect_tool_call=true` (checks that the model returns a structured tool call payload vs plain text for our tool-call adapter).

Interfaces / CLI: New flags: `--quality-spotcheck` (bool), `--spotcheck-prompts prompts/spotcheck/v1/prompts.jsonl`, `--spotcheck-sample-size 20` (default 20), `--spotcheck-seed`, `--spotcheck-concurrency 2` (default 2), `--spotcheck-fail-mode warn|hard` (default warn), `--spotcheck-timeout-ms 60000`. Environment: `EVAL_HARNESS_SPOTCHECK=1` enables spot-check in environments where wiring flags is annoying (e.g., some perf-canary configs). Exit codes: `warn` mode always exits 0 but emits a summary and marks failing cases in the artifact; `hard` mode exits non-zero if any prompt fails sanity checks.

Artifacts: Spot-check writes a small JSONL artifact alongside the perf artifacts (for dashboard drill-down and to attach to run manifests). Each line is a single case with: prompt_id, mode (chat/completion), model, region (if present in env), seed, decoding_params hash, ok, checks_failed, finish_reason, output_len_chars, output_len_tokens (when tokenizer available), latency_ms, and a truncated output preview (first ~300 chars) for quick triage. Example snippet (illustrative): `{ "prompt_id": "tool_call_weather_003", "ok": false, "checks_failed": "tool_call_missing;finish_reason_missing", "latency_ms": 412, "output_preview": "Sure—here’s the weather..." }`.

Implementation details: Introduces `SpotcheckRunner` with a narrow interface so benchmark-lab/perf-canary can call it without pulling in perf measurement code paths. Sampling is stable: we shuffle prompt IDs with a seeded RNG and take the first N; prompts can be filtered by mode. Tokenization is optional (if the backend exposes tokenizer info we use it; otherwise repetition checks fall back to character-gram heuristics). All checks are designed to be O(n) over the output and cheap vs a real eval.

Why it won’t contaminate perf results: By default, spot-check runs after the perf workload completes. It uses a separate client instance with its own request IDs and emits metrics under a different namespace/tag set so dashboards can separate perf measurements vs spot-check runs. If you intentionally want to interleave spot-check with perf (not recommended), you can pass `--spotcheck-phase pre` or `--spotcheck-phase interleave` but both are explicitly marked experimental in docs.

Docs: Adds `docs/spotcheck.md` describing usage, typical failure modes, and how to use the artifacts in triage. Also documents the privacy stance (no prod prompt content; prompts are synthetic/handwritten) and intended scope (“sanity checks, not a quality score”).

Tests: Unit tests for prompt parsing/validation, deterministic sampling, JSON parse expectation, tool-call expectation, and repetition guard logic. Added a small integration test with a mocked backend to ensure warn vs hard exit behavior and artifact emission.

Commits (squash-merged): (1) add spotcheck runner + CLI plumbing, (2) add prompt set v1 + schema/validator, (3) add sanity checks and artifact writer, (4) docs + tests, (5) address review feedback (determinism + fail-mode semantics).

Files / areas changed (high level): `src/spotcheck/runner.py`, `src/spotcheck/checks.py`, `src/spotcheck/prompts.py`, `src/cli.py` (flag wiring), `prompts/spotcheck/v1/prompts.jsonl`, `prompts/spotcheck/v1/schema.json`, `docs/spotcheck.md`, `tests/test_spotcheck_*`.

Review notes / discussion highlights: Claire Dubois: requested that the sampling be explicitly tied to the run manifest seed to support reproducibility across reruns; updated so `--spotcheck-seed` defaults to `RUN_MANIFEST.seed` when present, with an explicit log line. Rina Kobayashi: concerned about spot-check affecting perf runs on shared benchmark fleets; moved default phase to post-perf and added a clear warning + tag separation. Dr. Maya Srinivasan: requested stricter language in docs about what this is and isn’t, and confirmation that prompts are non-sensitive; added privacy note and removed an earlier draft prompt that was too close to a real customer workflow. Mateo Alvarez / Sofia Ivanova: asked that hard failures be opt-in until we have baseline failure rates; set default fail-mode to warn and added guidance for when to flip to hard (release gating).

CI: Initially failed `lint-python` due to unused import in checks module; fixed in follow-up commit. Final checks green: unit tests, lint, typecheck, packaging.

Outcome: Merged (squash) into main. Follow-ups: After this lands, perf-canary will wire `--quality-spotcheck` for nightly runs in warn mode for a calibration period; we’ll propose moving release-branch gating to hard mode once we have a stable baseline failure rate and a small allowlist of known-benign flakes.

Sampling is deterministic based on `--spotcheck-seed` (default ties to the perf run seed if present). No production prompt content is included. (3) Sanity checks (fast heuristics): non-empty output; UTF-8 decodes; no immediate server-side error indicators; maximum repetition guard (token-level repetition ratio threshold) to catch “stuck loop” regressions; finish_reason presence for supported backends; optional JSON parse check when the prompt declares `expect_json=true`; optional tool-call structure check when the prompt declares `expect_tool_call=true` (checks that the model returns a structured tool call payload vs plain text for our tool-call adapter).

Interfaces / CLI: New flags: `--quality-spotcheck` (bool), `--spotcheck-prompts prompts/spotcheck/v1/prompts.jsonl`, `--spotcheck-sample-size 20` (default 20), `--spotcheck-seed`, `--spotcheck-concurrency 2` (default 2), `--spotcheck-fail-mode warn|hard` (default warn), `--spotcheck-timeout-ms 60000`. Environment: `EVAL_HARNESS_SPOTCHECK=1` enables spot-check in environments where wiring flags is annoying (e.g., some perf-canary configs). Exit codes: `warn` mode always exits 0 but emits a summary and marks failing cases in the artifact; `hard` mode exits non-zero if any prompt fails sanity checks.

Artifacts: Spot-check writes a small JSONL artifact alongside the perf artifacts (for dashboard drill-down and to attach to run manifests). Each line is a single case with: prompt_id, mode (chat/completion), model, region (if present in env), seed, decoding_params hash, ok, checks_failed, finish_reason, output_len_chars, output_len_tokens (when tokenizer available), latency_ms, and a truncated output preview (first ~300 chars) for quick triage. Example snippet (illustrative): `{ "prompt_id": "tool_call_weather_003", "ok": false, "checks_failed": "tool_call_missing;finish_reason_missing", "latency_ms": 412, "output_preview": "Sure—here’s the weather..." }`.

Implementation details: Introduces `SpotcheckRunner` with a narrow interface so benchmark-lab/perf-canary can call it without pulling in perf measurement code paths. Sampling is stable: we shuffle prompt IDs with a seeded RNG and take the first N; prompts can be filtered by mode. Tokenization is optional (if the backend exposes tokenizer info we use it; otherwise repetition checks fall back to character-gram heuristics). All checks are designed to be O(n) over the output and cheap vs a real eval.

Why it won’t contaminate perf results: By default, spot-check runs after the perf workload completes. It uses a separate client instance with its own request IDs and emits metrics under a different namespace/tag set so dashboards can separate perf measurements vs spot-check runs. If you intentionally want to interleave spot-check with perf (not recommended), you can pass `--spotcheck-phase pre` or `--spotcheck-phase interleave` but both are explicitly marked experimental in docs.

Docs: Adds `docs/spotcheck.md` describing usage, typical failure modes, and how to use the artifacts in triage. Also documents the privacy stance (no prod prompt content; prompts are synthetic/handwritten) and intended scope (“sanity checks, not a quality score”).

Tests: Unit tests for prompt parsing/validation, deterministic sampling, JSON parse expectation, tool-call expectation, and repetition guard logic. Added a small integration test with a mocked backend to ensure warn vs hard exit behavior and artifact emission.

Commits (squash-merged): (1) add spotcheck runner + CLI plumbing, (2) add prompt set v1 + schema/validator, (3) add sanity checks and artifact writer, (4) docs + tests, (5) address review feedback (determinism + fail-mode semantics).

Files / areas changed (high level): `src/spotcheck/runner.py`, `src/spotcheck/checks.py`, `src/spotcheck/prompts.py`, `src/cli.py` (flag wiring), `prompts/spotcheck/v1/prompts.jsonl`, `prompts/spotcheck/v1/schema.json`, `docs/spotcheck.md`, `tests/test_spotcheck_*`.

Review notes / discussion highlights: Claire Dubois: requested that the sampling be explicitly tied to the run manifest seed to support reproducibility across reruns; updated so `--spotcheck-seed` defaults to `RUN_MANIFEST.seed` when present, with an explicit log line. Rina Kobayashi: concerned about spot-check affecting perf runs on shared benchmark fleets; moved default phase to post-perf and added a clear warning + tag separation. Dr. Maya Srinivasan: requested stricter language in docs about what this is and isn’t, and confirmation that prompts are non-sensitive; added privacy note and removed an earlier draft prompt that was too close to a real customer workflow. Mateo Alvarez / Sofia Ivanova: asked that hard failures be opt-in until we have baseline failure rates; set default fail-mode to warn and added guidance for when to flip to hard (release gating).

CI: Initially failed `lint-python` due to unused import in checks module; fixed in follow-up commit. Final checks green: unit tests, lint, typecheck, packaging.

Outcome: Merged (squash) into main. Follow-ups: After this lands, perf-canary will wire `--quality-spotcheck` for nightly runs in warn mode for a calibration period; we’ll propose moving release-branch gating to hard mode once we have a stable baseline failure rate and a small allowlist of known-benign flakes.

title: Add quantization profile baseline selection (by model family + GPU SKU) for quality gates
preview: Context: Quality gates for Optimize need a stable, apples-to-apples baseline when evaluating quantization profile changes. Today, gate runs pick a baseline based on “current deployed config” which can vary by region / hardware and sometimes points at a different GPU SKU than the target (esp. when capacity causes temporary spillover). That makes quantization deltas noisy and occasionally wrong. This PR introduces explicit baseline selection for quantization profile diffs based on (model_family, gpu_sku) with guardrails, logging, and tests. What this adds: (1) Baseline selection logic for quantization gating: new BaselineSelector that resolves a baseline config for gate runs using model family + canonical GPU SKU + runtime-compat constraints (context window bucket + kv_cache_mode). (2) Baseline source precedence: a) exact match: (model_family, gpu_sku) pinned “golden baseline” entry; b) compatible fallback: same model_family on sku_class (e.g., "ampere"), if explicitly marked allow_fallback=true; c) last-resort: fp16_default baseline for the model_family with gate marked degraded=true and enforcement requiring “full gates” (smoke tier cannot pass degraded baselines). (3) Guardrails: blocks baseline selection if it would cross architecture boundaries (e.g., hopper -> ampere) unless allow_fallback is set AND quant profile is marked "arch_compatible"; blocks if baseline model version differs from the gate baseline version requested by Optimize (prevents comparing different weights). (4) Persistence + auditability: gate_run record now stores baseline_profile_id, baseline_reason, and baseline_selector_version. (5) Metrics/logs: emits optimize.gates.baseline_selection.{success,failure} with tags (model_family, gpu_sku, reason, degraded). (6) Tests: unit tests for selection matrix + integration test that verifies the eval-harness request payload includes baseline_profile_id and that degraded baselines force full gates. Why this is safe: It only affects gate evaluation inputs for quantization config diffs (no runtime inference path change). If selector cannot find a safe baseline, we fail the gate run early with an explicit error (BaselineSelectionError) rather than running a misleading comparison. Related work: - ENG-3924 (this PR) - pr-19511 (config diff schema/validation) - pr-19523 (Optimize -> eval-harness wiring) - eval-harness pr-418 (flake score / rerun policy) Notes: This intentionally does not auto-create baseline entries. Baseline registry is seeded from existing “known-good” configs per model family + SKU (see migration + fixtures). Follow-up: add a small admin tool to list missing baselines per region/SKU during rollout (separate PR).

title: local-registry-fast-sync-and-rollback-snapshots
preview: Motivation: Private on-prem installs frequently hit long bootstrap windows when seeding large OCI registries over slow links. This PR introduces a delta-aware fast-sync engine plus snapshot-based rollback anchors so customers can perform atomic upgrades and quickly revert to a known registry state in air-gapped or VPC environments. What changed: 1) new Terraform module redwood.local_registry that orchestrates an initial seeding plan and creates a signed snapshot manifest, 2) a sync agent (installer/bin/registry-sync) that performs checksum-driven delta pulls, resumes interrupted transfers, and applies layer-level parallelism tuned for low-latency paper networks, 3) snapshot manifest format and a lightweight anchor scheme (snap-<epoch>-<seq>) stored in the registry under a signed tag, 4) Helm chart additions: registry-mirror-init sidecar, offline-values configmap templating and a preflight hook to validate snapshot compatibility, 5) upgrade/rollback tools: installer/bin/rollback-to-snapshot that verifies chain integrity before promoting a snapshot atomically, 6) docs and runbooks under docs/installer/reg-sync.md and runbooks/rollback-playbook.md, 7) tests: e2e against a local OCI mirror and unit tests for manifest signing/verify. Rollback approach: snapshots are created before any upgrade step, indexed by monotonically increasing sequence and a signed digest. Rollback validates that the registry contains all referenced layers and performs an atomic tag swap using a temporary tag promotion flow to avoid partial reads. Backwards compatibility: the manifest schema v1 is backward compatible with our existing sealed bundle indexer; the Terraform module offers both a 'seed' and 'resume' mode. Testing and validation: ran offline e2e in a simulated 10Mbps link with large model blobs; median bootstrap time reduced 4.2x versus baseline, resume tests passed on network interruption, and rollback exercise completed within expected latency budget. Security considerations: manifests are signed with customer KMS keys when available; sidecar runs as non-root. Migration notes: existing install workflows that rely on the previous registry seeding script can opt-in to 'fast_sync=true' in the installer config. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module + docs - [x] sync agent binary + unit tests - [x] Helm chart hooks and templates - [x] rollback tool + runbook - [x] e2e validation and CI integration - [x] docs and example values How to validate locally: follow docs/installer/local-fast-sync.md to bring up a miniregistry, run terraform apply in examples/registry-seed, then run installer/bin/registry-sync --plan examples/plan.json. Related: follows discussions in INFRA-234 and reuses part of the delta-indexing code introduced in ENG-42099.

title: reconcile-stream-token-wrap-and-delimiter-collision-handling
preview: Motivation: Intermittent production incidents showed that when clients send rapid small SSE chunks from heterogeneous networks we sometimes emit adjacent delimiter tokens that collide with partial JSON fragments, causing consumer libraries to mistake boundary-free fragments for complete JSON units. This resulted in malformed structured output, duplicated function/tool-call sentinels, and occasional token-order inversion when fragments were reordered by the proxy. This PR introduces a deterministic token-wrap reconciliation layer and a lightweight delimiter disambiguator to avoid mid-JSON collision while preserving low-latency streaming. Changes include: 1) a sequenced anchor inserted at chunk boundaries (non-user-visible, opt-in via runtime flag) to allow reassembly without altering event semantics, 2) a delimiter disambiguator that rewrites ambiguous trailing delimiter runs into an internal guarded form (transparent to OpenAI-compat consumers), 3) preservation ordering policy that tags function-call sentinel candidates with sequence stamps so interleaved fragments reserialize in canonical order, 4) additional unit and fuzz tests exercising reordered and split-UTF8 scenarios, and 5) a small runtime flag and migration note for Dedicated clusters to opt-in before rollout. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-1458) [x] unit tests added [x] integration test harness with proxy reorder simulation [x] benchmark smoke to verify <1ms median added latency under typical loads [x] compatibility shim preserves existing OpenAI-compat tokens. Impact/risk: low surface-area change to stream emitter; could affect custom proxy middleware that depends on exact byte-level framing. Rollout: staged feature-flagged rollout with Canary and Dedicated opt-in, automatic fallback to old path on runtime errors. Related: ENG-1458, PRs: #361112 (stream-test-harness), #364001 (sse-proxy-sim).

title: Add quantization profile canary scenarios (baseline vs profile-enabled) with thresholds + bisect hints
preview: Summary: This PR adds perf-canary coverage for the Quantization Profile Library route-level enablement by introducing paired scenarios that run baseline (default precision) vs profile-enabled execution for the same model/workload mix, then compares deltas against explicit thresholds. The goal is to catch (a) tail-latency regressions, (b) decode throughput drops, (c) VRAM/caching regressions, and (d) new error modes (profile incompatibility causing unexpected fallback churn), before profiles are exposed to beta traffic. Motivation: With route-level quantization enablement landing across control plane + runtime, we need deterministic, continuously-running signals that quantify the risk of enabling a profile and that produce actionable failure output. These scenarios are intended to run on the same infra as existing decode/tps canaries and to surface “bisect hints” that point engineers to likely layers (policy resolution vs artifact fetch vs kernel path vs cache pressure). What’s included: (1) A new scenario type `quant_profile_compare` that executes two runs (baseline + enabled) using identical prompts and compares p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec, and error rate. (2) Scenario templates for two context regimes (short chat-style: 256 in / 256 out; long-context: 4k in / 512 out) to exercise KV cache behavior. (3) GPU matrix support (A100-80GB + H100-80GB) with per-SKU thresholds (H100 has stricter decode latency deltas due to kernel selection differences; A100 has stricter VRAM headroom thresholds). (4) Failure classification + automated bisect hints printed in the canary report, including common patterns: “metadata fetch spike / registry throttling”, “fallback rate elevated due to incompatibility”, “decode-only regression likely kernel path”, “prefill regression likely activation quantization / cache fragmentation”. (5) Wiring to export new comparison metrics so dashboards can slice by route/model/gpu and track trendlines over time. Notes/constraints: The scenarios default to running against a pinned model + pinned quantization_profile_ref in staging/perf-canary environments to avoid accidental drift. The scenario runner enforces determinism flags and pins seed where supported to reduce noise (still non-deterministic at token level; we only gate on perf/infra metrics). Thresholds were chosen based on last 14d baseline variance plus an additional buffer for multi-tenant noise; see `docs/quant_profile_compare.md` in this PR for rationale. Rollout: Add scenarios disabled-by-default behind `ENABLE_QUANT_PROFILE_CANARIES` until the control plane config endpoints are fully deployed in perf-canary. Once available, we’ll enable in staging for 48h, then production canary at low frequency before full schedule. Follow-ups: Add a third workload focused on tool-calling/structured output once the runtime path is fully aligned (out of scope here).

title: [LTS Backport] Fix scheduler starvation edge case under bursty traffic + add regression reproducer
preview:
Context
We hit an edge case during the LTS pilot where a small fraction of requests could sit in the scheduler queue for an unbounded amount of time under bursty traffic. This manifested as occasional multi-minute tail latency spikes even though the system was otherwise healthy (no crash, no OOM), and it was hard to catch in steady-state load tests.

Root cause (high level)
In the request admission loop, when a request fails admission due to transient capacity constraints (e.g., batch token budget / KV budget), we requeue it and retry soon. Under a specific burst pattern (short prompts arriving continuously while a few long-prefill requests repeatedly fail admission), the fairness cursor/deficit bookkeeping could keep selecting the same requeued long request near the head of its queue. Because we did not treat repeated admission failures as “yielding” events, the scheduler could effectively spin on the same request and starve other eligible requests behind it.

What changed
- Treat repeated admission-control failures as a yield: after N consecutive “no-admit” cycles for a request, we advance the queue cursor and charge the attempt against the fairness budget so that other work gets a chance.
- Add a small cap on immediate requeue retries for the same request (per-queue) to prevent hot-loop behavior when bursts align with token budget boundaries.
- Add explicit metrics to make this class of issue observable in LTS rollouts: scheduler_requeue_attempts_total and scheduler_no_admit_yields_total (behind existing runtime metrics plumbing).

Why safe for LTS
- Fix is localized to scheduling fairness bookkeeping and retry handling; no changes to kernel paths, model execution, or API surface.
- Behavior is strictly more fair under contention; under non-bursty workloads the change is effectively a no-op.
- Includes a deterministic regression test that fails on runtime-1.18-lts prior to this patch.

Test plan
- Added regression reproducer: SchedulerStarvationBurstyTrafficTest
  - Simulates bursts of short requests (mixed prompt lengths) while injecting long-prefill requests that intermittently fail admission.
  - Asserts: (1) all requests drain, (2) no request exceeds a bounded queue wait time, (3) fairness cursor advances when admission repeatedly fails.
- Unit: //runtime/scheduler/... (new test + existing suite)
- Release-branch smoke: scheduler regression suite enabled for runtime-1.18-lts
- Perf-canary: ran "lts-release-branch" benchmark workflow; no statistically significant p50/p95 regressions within thresholds. If anything, slight tail improvement under synthetic burst (within noise).

Notes
- This is a backport of the mainline fix originally introduced in #23392 (not a clean cherry-pick due to minor drift in metrics names on the LTS branch). Diff kept minimal.
- If we see any unexpected throughput impact on specific Dedicated deployments, we can tune the retry cap via existing scheduler config (defaults chosen conservatively).

Checklist
- [x] Backport rubric: Sev2 correctness/latency issue, low-risk localized change
- [x] Added regression test
- [x] CI + scheduler smoke suite
- [x] Perf gate run attached
- [x] SRE sign-off for rollout monitoring

Follow-ups (not for this PR)
- Consider adding a canary dashboard panel for scheduler_no_admit_yields_total correlated with queue_time_p95 during patch rollout.

- Treat repeated admission-control failures as a yield: after N consecutive “no-admit” cycles for a request, we advance the queue cursor and charge the attempt against the fairness budget so that other work gets a chance.
- Add a small cap on immediate requeue retries for the same request (per-queue) to prevent hot-loop behavior when bursts align with token budget boundaries.
- Add explicit metrics to make this class of issue observable in LTS rollouts: scheduler_requeue_attempts_total and scheduler_no_admit_yields_total (behind existing runtime metrics plumbing).

Why safe for LTS
- Fix is localized to scheduling fairness bookkeeping and retry handling; no changes to kernel paths, model execution, or API surface.
- Behavior is strictly more fair under contention; under non-bursty workloads the change is effectively a no-op.
- Includes a deterministic regression test that fails on runtime-1.18-lts prior to this patch.

Test plan
- Added regression reproducer: SchedulerStarvationBurstyTrafficTest
  - Simulates bursts of short requests (mixed prompt lengths) while injecting long-prefill requests that intermittently fail admission.
  - Asserts: (1) all requests drain, (2) no request exceeds a bounded queue wait time, (3) fairness cursor advances when admission repeatedly fails.
- Unit: //runtime/scheduler/... (new test + existing suite)
- Release-branch smoke: scheduler regression suite enabled for runtime-1.18-lts
- Perf-canary: ran "lts-release-branch" benchmark workflow; no statistically significant p50/p95 regressions within thresholds. If anything, slight tail improvement under synthetic burst (within noise).

Notes
- This is a backport of the mainline fix originally introduced in #23392 (not a clean cherry-pick due to minor drift in metrics names on the LTS branch). Diff kept minimal.
- If we see any unexpected throughput impact on specific Dedicated deployments, we can tune the retry cap via existing scheduler config (defaults chosen conservatively).

Checklist
- [x] Backport rubric: Sev2 correctness/latency issue, low-risk localized change
- [x] Added regression test
- [x] CI + scheduler smoke suite
- [x] Perf gate run attached
- [x] SRE sign-off for rollout monitoring

Follow-ups (not for this PR)
- Consider adding a canary dashboard panel for scheduler_no_admit_yields_total correlated with queue_time_p95 during patch rollout.

title: hotfix(lts): out-of-band security patch for runtime-1.18-lts (safe archive extraction + comms checklist)
preview:
Context: This is an out-of-band security hotfix for the Runtime LTS line (runtime-1.18-lts). We received a private report that our model artifact cache extraction path could be abused if a malicious/compromised artifact contains symlinks or path traversal entries ("../") that escape the intended extraction directory. This primarily impacts Private deployments that use customer-provided artifact registries and any environment where artifacts can be injected into the cache layer. This PR hardens archive extraction and adds explicit deny rules + tests. It also includes the tightened approval + CI + comms checklist we agreed to for LTS security patches.

Severity / tracking: High (customer-controlled input path + potential file overwrite). CVE request in progress (internal tracking: SEC-2025-019); will update with CVE/GHSA once assigned. No customer exploit observed; patch is being cut under embargo.

What changed (code): 1) Hardened tar/zip extraction in runtime artifact cache: reject entries with absolute paths, parent directory traversal, or Windows drive prefixes; do not follow symlinks/hardlinks; enforce maximum file count and per-file size caps; require all extracted paths to remain within the target directory after realpath normalization. 2) Added a "safe_extract" helper with centralized validation and audit logging (log-only in debug builds; redacted paths in production logs). 3) Added unit tests covering traversal, symlink escape, and archive bombs. 4) Added a targeted integration test that runs in release-branch CI (fast) to ensure the cache directory remains unchanged outside extraction root.

Operational notes: No perf impact expected; extraction is not on token-critical path. The stricter validation can cause artifact download failures if a customer artifact is malformed; error message includes a stable error code (RUNTIME_ARTIFACT_EXTRACT_VALIDATION_FAILED) for Support triage.

Release intent: Cut runtime-1.18.1-lts+security.1 (tag format per ADR-027 draft; final tag to be created by release manager). This patch should be made available to Dedicated and Private customers on the LTS channel as "recommended" and may be escalated to "mandatory" based on Security review.

Expedited LTS security patch checklist (must be completed before merge): [x] Security sign-off (Dr. Aisha Rahman delegate / Hanae Suzuki) [x] Runtime sign-off (Noah Patel + Mateo Alvarez or delegate) [x] Release Eng sign-off (Connor O'Brien) [x] SRE sign-off for rollout/alerting (Sean Gallagher) [x] CI: release-branch unit + integration suite green [x] CI: scheduler smoke tests green [x] CI: container build + vulnerability scan green [x] Perf gates: waive full perf-canary; run "perf-smoke" set and document results (see below) [x] Artifact provenance: generate checksums + provenance metadata (handled in release pipeline; verified in staging) [x] Comms: draft internal Support/CS heads-up and customer advisory stub (without exploit details) [x] Backport notes: confirm no additional cherry-picks required

Perf-smoke results (A100, H100; 5 runs; p95): Within noise vs baseline (+0.2% to +0.6% p95 latency), throughput unchanged. Full perf-canary will run post-tag as informational.

Files / areas touched: - runtime/artifacts/safe_extract.{cc,h} (new helper) - runtime/artifacts/model_cache.cc (use safe_extract + error codes) - runtime/tests/artifacts/safe_extract_test.cc (new) - ci/workflows/release-branch.yml (add extraction integration test) - docs/internal/security/lts-hotfix-comms-checklist.md (new internal doc for on-call/release manager)

Rollout / comms plan: 1) Publish artifacts to LTS channel with signed checksums. 2) Notify Support/CS in #support and #customer-success with patch availability + error code mapping + recommended next steps. 3) For Private customers, provide advisory + upgrade guidance (pin to patch, verify checksums, rollback steps). 4) Dedicated: phased rollout per region with elevated alerting; rollback on elevated 5xx/latency anomalies.

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) path normalization correctness across platforms, (b) symlink/hardlink handling, (c) error messages not leaking sensitive paths, and (d) CI gating coverage.

Follow-ups (not in this hotfix): - Add SBOM attestation to LTS artifacts (tracked in PR-23402) - Expand safe_extract to support additional archive formats we may introduce later - Wire Support playbook to include the new error code mapping

Refs: ENG-4829 (security patch SLA for LTS), ENG-4821 (LTS initiative epic), SUP-18506 (Private customer asks for checksums/pinning), INT-3302 (release branch secrets/permissions).

title: Runtime: load quantization profile artifacts, apply mode, and emit observability signals
preview: Summary: This PR wires the serving runtime to (1) consume the resolved quantization profile reference from routing metadata, (2) fetch and validate the corresponding profile metadata + artifacts from the model registry, (3) apply quantization mode deterministically for the request/model shard, and (4) emit metrics + structured events so rollouts can be observed and debugged. Motivation: The Quantization Profile Library is opt-in and route-scoped. For v1 we need deterministic selection, safe fallback, and clear signals for SRE and customers to verify whether a profile was applied for a given request. This PR is scoped to runtime behavior only; control plane resolution/compatibility checks are handled upstream (see related PRs). Design overview: - Input contract: router attaches quantization selection metadata to the request context (grpc metadata + internal request proto field). Runtime treats this as advisory and re-validates profile/model compatibility against the local model + GPU runtime capabilities. - Profile loading: new QuantProfileManager loads profile metadata via registry client (cached), then downloads artifact bundle (signed) to a local content-addressed cache. Artifacts are keyed by (profile_id, artifact_sha256) to guarantee determinism and avoid alias drift. - Deterministic application: on each request, runtime resolves outcome = {disabled|applied|fallback} with a single pass decision. If artifacts are missing/invalid/incompatible, runtime falls back to baseline precision for that model variant and records the reason code. No retry loops. - Runtime integration: ModelExecutor now accepts an optional QuantizationModeSpec derived from the profile. Kernel selection remains in kernel auto-selector; runtime passes a constrained spec and relies on kernel guardrails to reject unsupported mixes. - Observability: Adds counters and histograms for enablement outcome, artifact cache behavior, load latencies, and per-request annotations (logs + traces) including quant_profile_id and resolution outcome. Metrics are low-cardinality by design; profile_id is recorded in logs/traces but not as a metrics label. Metrics/events added: - Counter: runtime.quant_profile.requests_total{outcome=applied|fallback|disabled, reason=...} - Counter: runtime.quant_profile.artifact_fetch_total{result=success|error, source=cache|registry} - Histogram: runtime.quant_profile.artifact_fetch_latency_ms - Histogram: runtime.quant_profile.profile_resolve_latency_us - Gauge: runtime.quant_profile.cache_entries (debug-only, exported behind internal flag) - Structured event (json log): quant_profile_resolution with fields {request_id, route_id, model_id, model_variant, quant_profile_id, outcome, reason, artifact_sha256} - Trace attribute: redwood.quant_profile.id, redwood.quant_profile.outcome, redwood.quant_profile.reason Safety/guardrails: - Default behavior unchanged when routing metadata is absent or explicitly indicates "default". - Fallback is always baseline precision; never attempts to select an alternate quant profile in-runtime. - All profile fetches are rate-limited and cached; failures do not fail the request unless the request explicitly mandates a profile (not supported in v1; we treat all as best-effort). - Validates artifact signature + sha256 match before loading. - Adds circuit-breaker style suppression: repeated registry errors degrade to fallback without spamming registry. Testing: - Unit tests: profile parsing/validation, artifact cache keying, determinism invariants, reason code coverage. - Integration test: fake registry + runtime exec path ensures a quant_profile_id flows through request context -> applied -> annotated logs. - Perf smoke: decode path overhead measured to be <0.3% in microbench; artifact load occurs on first hit only. Rollout plan: This lands behind the existing feature gate in runtime config (QuantProfileRuntimeEnabled). When disabled, all new code paths short-circuit at the request boundary. Observability will be used in perf-canary and early beta routes to verify applied/fallback rates and to correlate latency/cost deltas. Related: - ENG-5816 runtime integration - ENG-5817 dashboards/metrics follow-up - pr-18427 policy compatibility guardrails (upstream) - pr-18445 control plane endpoints (upstream) Notes: This PR intentionally does not implement the global kill-switch (tracked separately). It also does not add profile_id as a metric label to avoid cardinality issues; profile granularity is available via logs/traces and audit events upstream.

title: runtime: kernel auto-selection guardrails for mixed INT8/FP8 quantization profiles
preview:
Summary: Update runtime kernel auto-selection to safely support quantization profiles that mix INT8 weights with FP8 compute (and related variants), while explicitly guarding against unsupported GPU/arch combos and problematic context-length regimes. This PR adds a capability matrix, hard validation + fallback paths, and a small set of microbench baselines to catch regressions.

Context: As part of the Quantization Profile Library workstream, we are starting to publish profiles that use INT8 weight-only or INT8+FP8 mixed paths. Prior kernel selection logic could (a) pick an FP8 decode kernel when only prefill was eligible, (b) attempt to enable FP8 kernels on unsupported SKUs (or driver/runtime combinations), and (c) fail “open” in rare cases (resulting in repeated selection attempts and noisy errors). We need deterministic, conservative selection with explicit fallbacks to baseline kernels.

What changed: (1) Added `QuantKernelCaps` capability resolution keyed by GPU sku class + compute capability + driver/runtime gates. (2) Introduced explicit “unsupported combo” guards for INT8 weight-only + FP8 activation paths on non-Hopper (e.g., A100) and on L4-class GPUs. (3) Added a context-length guardrail: for mixed INT8/FP8 profiles, cap FP8 decode kernels at max_ctx <= 16k until we complete KV cache fragmentation work; above the cap we select the INT8-safe decode path (or baseline if unavailable). (4) Tightened determinism: selection is now a pure function of (model family, quant profile, resolved caps, seq_len bucket, kv_cache_mode). We also annotate selection outcome for downstream metrics (`kernel_path=int8_w8a16|fp8_mixed|baseline_fallback`). (5) Added unit tests for the capability matrix + selection invariants, and a microbench script target used in perf-canary to sanity-check throughput/latency deltas.

Why this is safe: The default behavior remains unchanged when quantization profiles are disabled. When enabled, we choose the most specific supported kernel path and otherwise fall back to the baseline (non-quant) kernel family or to the INT8-safe path, with a single logged reason code per model load (not per request) to avoid log spam.

Notes: This PR intentionally does not introduce any new public API surface. It’s runtime-only logic used after the control plane resolves a profile. Follow-up work will extend perf-canary scenarios (ENG-5820) and expand coverage for max_ctx > 16k once KV cache work lands.

Microbench snippet (local bench; batch=1 decode, prefill=2k unless noted; tokens/s is median over 50 iters): H100-SXM (CUDA 12.4): llama-3-70b (decode 256): baseline 132 tok/s; int8_w8a16 170 tok/s (+28.8%); int8+fp8_mixed 186 tok/s (+40.9%). H100-SXM (prefill 2k): baseline 6,240 tok/s; int8_w8a16 7,910 tok/s (+26.8%); int8+fp8_mixed 8,330 tok/s (+33.5%). L40S (CUDA 12.4): mixed path correctly guarded -> selected int8_w8a16; baseline 88 tok/s; int8_w8a16 108 tok/s (+22.7%). A100-80GB: mixed path guarded -> baseline fallback for fp8 kernels; baseline 94 tok/s; int8_w8a16 116 tok/s (+23.4%). Tail latency (p95) on H100 remained within +1.6% for decode 256, and improved ~-4.2% for prefill due to reduced kernel thrash in the selector.

Testing: unit tests `runtime_kernel_select_test` + integration smoke `runtime_model_load_smoke` + bench target `tools/runtime_microbench`. CI includes gpu-sim checks only; perf numbers are from local bench + internal lab run referenced in comments.

Rollout: No rollout flags changed here. Selection changes only apply when a resolved quant profile requests a mixed path. We will canary under perf-canary + 1% design partner traffic once profile publication begins.

title: Disable experimental batching heuristic on runtime-1.18-lts (predictability > peak throughput)
preview:
Context: LTS customers (Dedicated/Private) have been clear that they want predictable latency and stable scheduling behavior across patches. The experimental batching heuristic we enabled on main in late 1.18 ("adaptive length bucket + opportunistic merge") does improve aggregate throughput on a subset of workloads, but it also increases run-to-run variance and can amplify p95 tail latency under bursty mixed-length traffic. For LTS we want fewer moving parts and more deterministic behavior by default.

What this does: On runtime-1.18-lts, default the experimental heuristic OFF and guard it behind an explicit opt-in flag. This keeps the codepath available for debugging/controlled experiments without making it part of the supported LTS default behavior.

Changes:
- LTS defaults: `scheduler.batching.experimental_heuristic = false`.
- Added explicit opt-in via config/env: `RW_RUNTIME_ENABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_BATCHING_HEURISTIC=1` (docs included). Note: on non-LTS branches the existing default remains unchanged.
- Added targeted scheduler regression test covering mixed prompt lengths + burst arrivals to ensure the stable path remains stable.
- Added a release-note blurb for LTS patch notes generator (throughput tradeoff + guidance).

Perf impact (perf-canary summary attached in CI):
- Mixed-length chat (bursty): p95 improved ~3-8% and variance reduced (fewer oscillations across runs).
- Long-form generation (steady load): throughput decreased ~2-6% depending on model/GPU (expected).
- No change in correctness outputs; only scheduling/batching decisions.

Why not remove entirely? Keeping it behind a flag helps if a customer explicitly wants it and accepts the tradeoffs, and it allows us to reproduce issues from main when triaging backports.

Rollout/ops: This is intended for the first LTS patch line. Support/CS should treat the opt-in as "best-effort / not recommended" unless Release Eng + SRE sign off for a given account.

Refs: ENG-4821 (Runtime LTS initiative), ENG-4831 (scheduler smoke tests).

title: Create runtime-1.18 LTS release branch scaffolding + tagging conventions
preview:
Context
We’re standing up the Runtime LTS channel to support Dedicated + Private customers that need a stable baseline for 6–12+ months. This PR lands the minimum repo-level scaffolding needed to cut and maintain the first LTS line based on runtime 1.18, plus conventions that downstream tooling (CI, artifact publishing, Private installer/Helm pinning) can rely on.

Important note: GitHub branch creation happens via repo settings; this PR adds the code/config that *assumes* we will create/protect `runtime-1.18-lts` and related tags. After merge, Release Eng will create the branch from the `runtime/v1.18.0` cut point and lock it down (required checks + required reviewers).

What’s included
- Branching + naming conventions:
  - LTS branch name: `runtime-1.18-lts`
  - Tag format: `runtime-1.18.<patch>-lts` (e.g., `runtime-1.18.0-lts`, `runtime-1.18.1-lts`)
  - Container tag format (runtime image): `runtime:1.18.<patch>-lts` (plus immutable digest in release artifacts)
- Release branch CI wiring:
  - Adds a `release-branch.yml` workflow that runs on `runtime-*-lts` and `release/*` patterns with stricter defaults (no flaky/optional gates).
  - Ensures we run deterministic build steps and publish a staging artifact manifest (no public publish here; just the plumbing).
- Minimal build/publish config changes:
  - `build/versioning` updated to accept the `-lts` suffix and keep semver ordering sane for tooling.
  - Adds a release artifact manifest format (checksums and commit SHA placeholders; provenance/signing added in follow-ups).
- Documentation:
  - `docs/release-process/runtime-lts.md` with the above conventions + “what to do after this PR merges” checklist.

Out of scope (follow-ups)
- Backport automation + labeling (ENG-4823 / PR #23201)
- Release notes generator “LTS mode” (ENG-4826)
- Artifact signing/provenance + SBOM generation (ENG-4832)
- Perf canary integration and thresholds enforcement (ENG-4825)

Validation
- Ran the new workflow against this branch with `DRY_RUN=1` to verify manifest generation and tagging logic.
- Verified the version parser change doesn’t affect mainline tags (unit tests included).

Rollout / Operational notes
- After merge:
  1) Create `runtime-1.18-lts` from the agreed 1.18 cut SHA (see checklist in docs).
  2) Enable branch protections: required checks = `release-branch / build`, `release-branch / unit`, `release-branch / smoke`.
  3) Require approvals from Runtime + Release Eng CODEOWNERS for changes to `runtime-1.18-lts`.
  4) Announce internally in #eng-releases when protections are live.

Risks
- Tag naming: we are standardizing now to avoid downstream churn (Private/Helm pinning semantics depend on this). If we need to change it, do so before the first patch is published.
- CI capacity: release-branch runs heavier jobs; SRE/Infra tracking capacity via INT-3302.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4821, ENG-4822, ENG-4824
- ADR (pending final publish): ADR-027 Runtime LTS branching/versioning
- Related threads: #eng-releases (branch naming), #eng-infra (secrets/permissions)

Checklist
- [x] Add release-branch workflow for LTS patterns
- [x] Add docs for branch + tag conventions
- [x] Update version parser to accept -lts
- [x] Confirm CODEOWNERS coverage for release process docs
- [ ] (Post-merge) Create/protect `runtime-1.18-lts` branch in GitHub
- [ ] (Post-merge) Wire artifact publish job to signing/provenance (ENG-4832)

title: introduce-prefill-sentinel-compactizer-and-cli-for-tenant-econ
preview: Motivation: tenant warmstart and prefill heuristics currently produce suboptimal cache utilization for low-cardinality workloads which inflates per-token cost. This PR adds a lightweight runtime 'prefill sentinel' that compacts low-value KV entries and a companion CLI to synthesize tenant prefill profiles and surface conservative quantization suggestions. The goal is to reduce tail token spend and improve contiguous cache hit rates without changing public API behavior. Implementation summary: added a sentinel compactizer algorithm in the runtime prefill path to detect stale/low-reuse KV prefixes, a tenant-level sampler and scoring function to derive compacting thresholds, a compact-while-warmup mode for Dedicated instances, metrics/tracing hooks for hit-rate impact, and a small CLI tool (prefillctl) that generates recommended batch/cache/quant presets from historical traces. This single-string description contains the full PR context, design tradeoffs, testing notes, and rollout plan: design decisions (heuristic thresholding, safety fallthroughs, per-tenant opt-out), testing (unit tests for compactizer, integration smoke in benchmark-lab with 100k synthetic requests, manual QA on staging Dedicated), rollback plan (feature flag disable and config revert), rollout (canary on 3 tenants -> 15% -> 50% -> global), and backward-compatibility assurances.

title: Phase-locked traffic replayer and quantile-hashmap serializer
preview:
Motivation: We frequently observe ephemeral microbursts that escape our existing replayers because sequence timing jitter masks phase relationships across concurrent sessions. This PR introduces a phase-locked loop (PLL) inspired traffic replayer that aligns request phases across synthetic sessions to deterministically reproduce multi-tenant interference patterns and kernel saturation events. It also adds a compact quantile-hashmap serializer for per-epoch quantile sketches so reporting pipelines can stream delta-updates without holding large in-memory sketches.

What this PR does (single-string summary): Adds a new lightweight replayer (bench/replayers/phase_locked_replayer.py) that can lock multiple synthetic sessions to a scheduler phase, implements deterministic seeding and jitter maps, exposes an API to pin phase offsets and slot widths, and integrates with the existing bench-run harness. Adds a quantile-hashmap serializer (bench/reporting/quantile_hashmap.py) which converts DDSketch-style buckets into a small hashmap keyed by quantile-range + epoch with fast merge/serialize semantics. Adds end-to-end perf tests and reporting pipelines to the `bench-run` CI job and a smoke dashboard exporter to persist compacted quantiles to the S3-compatible artifact store.

Why this is different: Existing replayers randomize inter-request spacing or replay wall-clock traces; the PLL replayer models phase coherence explicitly so we can reproduce pathological alignments (e.g., slot-synchronized bursts) which historically caused 2-3x p99 regressions on certain kernels. The quantile-hashmap serializer makes per-epoch quantile diffs cheap to emit, enabling finer-grained alerting without heavy storage or downstream aggregation work.

Checklist: 
- [x] New replayer implementation with deterministic tests
- [x] Quantile serializer with merge/serialize unit tests
- [x] Integration: bench-run job updated to exercise phase-locked scenarios
- [x] Examples: small scenario config and a README describing modes and knob semantics
- [x] Documentation: short design note added to docs/bench-lab/phase-locked.md

Interfaces and knobs: 
- replayer: bench.replayers.phase_locked.PhaseLockedReplayer(phase_period_ms, slot_width_ms, seed, jitter_map)
- run mode: bench-run --scenario phase-locked --concurrency 64 --pin-phase-offset 17
- serializer: QuantileHashmap(epoch_id, sketch_buckets) -> bytes (compact streamable diff)

High-level performance validation: 
- Synthetic reproduction of a previously flaky kernel-hotpath showed identical flamepoints across runs with locked phases (reproducibility > 99% for p99 hotspots).
- Storage: quantile-hashmap serializations reduced per-epoch telemetry size by ~6x vs full DDSketch exports for our default bucket set.

Included small illustrative code excerpt (for reviewers only):
- Phase locking loop core (pseudo):
  for phase_tick in range(0, duration_ms, phase_period_ms):
    for slot in slots:
      scheduled_time = phase_tick + (slot.index * slot_width_ms) + jitter_map[slot.id]
      schedule_request(session_for_slot(slot), scheduled_time)

Commit summary and intent is in the commits field below. All new code is feature-flagged behind `ENABLE_PHASE_REPLAYER` and defaults to off in prod runs.

Files of interest changed are listed below; diffs are small and focused. CI and perf runs were added to protect regressions.

title: fleet-granularity-autoscaler-and-per-pool-minimum-throughput-guard
preview: Motivation: some fleets with mixed pool sizes and bursty tenants were observing transient drops in per-tenant throughput when the autoscaler downscaled coarse-grained slots. This PR introduces a new "fleet granularity autoscaler" that reasons about per-pool minimum throughput floors and a lightweight ephemeral micro-slot reservation to absorb short microbursts while preserving long-term fairness. The change has three parts: 1) allocator: introduce per-pool minimum throughput guards and a new soft-reservation API for ephemeral micro-slots; 2) autoscaler: a granularity-aware scale decision that penalizes downscales that would violate pool floors and adjusts target replica counts using a weighted hysteresis; 3) telemetry: emit a new sampled utilization heatmap and per-pool min-throughput breach counter to drive alerting and future ML-based tuning. Implementation notes: the autoscaler uses a 3-window moving median of observed throughput per pool, computes candidate downscale deltas, and simulates micro-slot assignment to ensure long-tail tenants are not catastrophically impacted. The soft-reservation path is conservative by default and enabled behind feature-flag rw.feature.fleet_granularity. Backwards compatibility: existing reservation and burst token flows remain unchanged; the new soft-reservation is additive and only used for transient requests. Tests: unit tests for allocation logic, integration tests for autoscaler decisions, and an end-to-end e2e that runs a synthetic burst profile against a three-pool cluster. Rollout plan: feature-flag gated, monitor new telemetry for 72 hours behind canary ratio 10% then ramp. Checklist: - [x] Implement allocator guards and soft-reservation API - [x] Add autoscaler decision logic and hysteresis tuning - [x] Emit telemetry and wire to prometheus metrics - [x] Unit and integration tests - [x] e2e test and manual canary validation - [x] Docs (ops runbook and rwctl helper) Links: Linear ENG-1123 for requirement, SUP-4421 for customer incident repro and logs.
