title: Introduce lifecycle signatures and staged compatibility snippets for onboarding changelogs
preview:
Background: onboarding churn and subtle compatibility regressions have made changelogs noisy and hard to consume. We need a small, machine-readable lifecycle signal attached to model profiles so release automation can produce concise, actionable changelog snippets when a model moves between lifecycle phases (onboard -> staging -> prod). This PR introduces 'lifecycle_signature' metadata on model profiles and a staged compatibility-snippet generator that outputs human-friendly changelog fragments and suggested fallback mappings for deployments.

What this PR does: introduces a new lightweight lifecycle_signature schema (versioned), adds an onboarding hook to compute the signature, adds a changelog assembler plugin that emits staged compatibility snippets, and wires a CLI flag to preview generated snippets. The generator uses a deterministic fingerprint of (architecture, tokenizer_hash, primary_quant, perf_token_summary) and a short delta heuristic to summarize compatibility impact and performance deltas.

Checklist:
- [x] lifecycle_signature schema and migration support
- [x] onboarding hook: registry/hooks/lifecycle_signer.py
- [x] changelog assembler plugin: registry/hooks/compat_snippets.py
- [x] CLI preview: registry/cli/onboard_preview.py --staged-snippets
- [x] unit tests for signature calculation and snippet heuristics
- [x] docs: docs/onboard-lifecycle.md with examples and rollout guidance

Design notes: the lifecycle_signature is intentionally compact (hex-encoded 12 bytes + version tag) to remain stable in manifests and to avoid bloating release bundles. The staged compatibility snippet is a short paragraph and a suggested remap table (previous_alias -> recommended_variant) to help platform teams stage rollouts. The heuristics aim for conservative suggestions (only propose remaps when the signature delta + perf delta exceed thresholds).

Why this is different from profile hashes: profile hashes are content fingerprints; lifecycle_signature encodes a small set of compatibility-relevant attributes and a stability score to be used by release tooling and console UI for clearer onboarding narratives.

Backward compatibility and migration: existing profiles without lifecycle_signature are treated as 'unknown' and will get a computed signature on the next onboard. The migration is non-blocking; the assembler falls back to legacy blurb generation if signature is absent.

Operational considerations: the plugin adds a single CPU-bound computation during onboarding and a fast, cached path in changelog assembly. We added a short TTL cache keyed by model_id+head_sha to avoid recomputation for large batch uploads.

Risks and mitigations: initial heuristics may be conservative and under-suggest remaps; we added a monitoring metric (registry.lifecycle.signer.suggestions.count) and a small sampling telemetry to evaluate precision.

How to test locally: run `pytest tests/test_lifecycle_signatures.py::test_signature_determinism` and `python -m registry.cli.onboard_preview --file sample_profile.json --staged-snippets` to see generated snippet output.

Related: ENG-7421 (project to tighten onboarding churn), PM-318 (product request for clearer release notes).

title: Introduce onboarding contract mapper and traffic-weighted release-notes generator
preview:
Context / motivation: model onboarding churn has made release notes noisy: small metadata-only updates (typo fixes, curator comments) and large perf-affecting changes appear at the same verbosity level. Teams want concise, signal-rich changelogs that call out performance and runtime compatibility changes while suppressing low-impact edits. This PR adds two complementary pieces: (1) an onboarding contract mapper that normalizes and stamps the metadata that matters for serving/runtime compatibility, and (2) a traffic-weighted release-notes generator that scores deltas based on historical usage and staged rollout traffic to prioritize changelog sections.

What this PR does (high level):
- Introduce a lightweight onboarding_contract_mapper that: canonicalizes fields (model.aliases, quantization_profiles, runtime_hints), emits a stability fingerprint, and records a \"compatibility_contract\" object (stringified map) that downstream systems use for gating. The mapper runs in the intake path and as a pre-commit hook for model manifests.
- Add a traffic_weighted_changelog generator service that: consumes delta events and model traffic signals (ingest from telemetry snapshots), computes an impact_score that weights changes by percent-traffic and latency/cost delta, and outputs a compact release note with prioritized bullets (Performance, Runtime Compatibility, Behavioral, Metadata). The generator also groups low-impact deltas into an \"Other metadata edits\" bucket to reduce noise.
- Provide migration tooling: a one-off migrator that rehydrates existing model profiles with compatibility_contract keys and generates one sample traffic-weighted release digest for the last 30 days (stored as samples/release-digest-*.ndjson).
- Add unit + integration tests and CI job to validate contract_mapper idempotence and changelog ranking monotonicity.

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- We intentionally keep the compatibility_contract as a stable, compact string map to avoid exploding DB schema changes; the contract is designed to be backward-compatible for a two-release window.
- Impact scoring is heuristic (traffic percent * normalized perf delta + deprecation_penalty + security_flag), not ML-driven; we opted for deterministic, explainable rules in this iteration to keep auditability simple.
- The changelog generator does not automatically publish to user-facing release pages; it emits drafts for the release curator UI to review first. Future work could hook canary rollout metrics for live weighting.

Checklist:
- [x] Mapper implementation with canonicalization rules
- [x] Compatibility fingerprint + stamping
- [x] Changelog generator with traffic weighting and bucketing rules
- [x] Migrator and sample output artifacts
- [x] Unit tests for mapper and generator scoring
- [x] Integration tests with sample telemetry snapshot
- [x] CI job added and passing

Commits summary: single-squash merge with these commit headlines:
- feat(onboard): add onboarding_contract_mapper and canonicalizer
- feat(changelog): implement traffic_weighted_changelog service and API
- chore(migration): add rehydrate migrator and sample output generator
- test: add unit/integration tests for mapper + changelog scoring
- ci: add changelog-scoring CI job and test fixtures

Changed areas / notable files (high level):
- src/ingest/contract_mapper.py -- core canonicalization logic and fingerprinting rules
- src/ingest/rules.yaml -- canonical field mappings and normalization rules (documented)
- src/changelog/weighting.py -- impact scoring and bucketing algorithm
- src/changelog/service.py -- API to produce draft release digests
- tools/migrators/rehydrate_compat_contract.py -- one-off migrator scaffolding
- tests/test_contract_mapper.py, tests/test_changelog_scoring.py -- test coverage for idempotence and monotonicity
- ci/workflows/changelog-scoring.yml -- new CI workflow validating scoring determinism

Review conversation (high level):
- Arielle Chen (review): \"Nice separation between contract and draft generator. Two asks: (1) make fingerprint stable across minor metadata edits, (2) add examples in rules.yaml for quant profiles.\"
- Author reply (Priya): \"Addressed: fingerprint uses a whitelist of fields and salt; added quant examples + unit tests that exercise the stability property.\"
- Mateo Alvarez (review): \"Can we avoid coupling telemetry ingestion directly in the changelog service? Suggest adding a small adapter interface so we can swap sources.\"
- Author reply: \"Added TelemetryAdapter interface and a simple in-memory adapter for the migrator; production wiring kept in ingestion config. Also added an integration test using the adapter.\"
- Jonah Beck (approval): \"Approach looks good; tests pass and migration artifacts useful for manual validation. Approving.\"

CI summary: all checks passed on merge. Notable checks:
- unit-tests (python): pass
- integration-tests (slow): pass (ran against test telemetry snapshot)
- lint: pass
- changelog-scoring-ci: pass (validates determinism across repeated runs)

Migration notes for operators: run tools/migrators/rehydrate_compat_contract.py --window 30d --out samples/ to generate sample release digests. The migrator writes ndjson artifacts and a dry-run log (migrations/log-48290-dryrun.txt). The migrator is idempotent; it will skip entries that already have a compatibility_contract stamp.

text:
Context / motivation: model onboarding churn has made release notes noisy: small metadata-only updates (typo fixes, curator comments) and large perf-affecting changes appear at the same verbosity level. Teams want concise, signal-rich changelogs that call out performance and runtime compatibility changes while suppressing low-impact edits. This PR adds two complementary pieces: (1) an onboarding contract mapper that normalizes and stamps the metadata that matters for serving/runtime compatibility, and (2) a traffic-weighted release-notes generator that scores deltas based on historical usage and staged rollout traffic to prioritize changelog sections.

What this PR does (high level):
- Introduce a lightweight onboarding_contract_mapper that: canonicalizes fields (model.aliases, quantization_profiles, runtime_hints), emits a stability fingerprint, and records a \"compatibility_contract\" object (stringified map) that downstream systems use for gating. The mapper runs in the intake path and as a pre-commit hook for model manifests.
- Add a traffic_weighted_changelog generator service that: consumes delta events and model traffic signals (ingest from telemetry snapshots), computes an impact_score that weights changes by percent-traffic and latency/cost delta, and outputs a compact release note with prioritized bullets (Performance, Runtime Compatibility, Behavioral, Metadata). The generator also groups low-impact deltas into an \"Other metadata edits\" bucket to reduce noise.
- Provide migration tooling: a one-off migrator that rehydrates existing model profiles with compatibility_contract keys and generates one sample traffic-weighted release digest for the last 30 days (stored as samples/release-digest-*.ndjson).
- Add unit + integration tests and CI job to validate contract_mapper idempotence and changelog ranking monotonicity.

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- We intentionally keep the compatibility_contract as a stable, compact string map to avoid exploding DB schema changes; the contract is designed to be backward-compatible for a two-release window.
- Impact scoring is heuristic (traffic percent * normalized perf delta + deprecation_penalty + security_flag), not ML-driven; we opted for deterministic, explainable rules in this iteration to keep auditability simple.
- The changelog generator does not automatically publish to user-facing release pages; it emits drafts for the release curator UI to review first. Future work could hook canary rollout metrics for live weighting.

title: implement-tf-module-gpu-nodepool-lease-index-and-iam-role-template-rotation
preview:
Context and motivation: Private deployments need consistent, deterministic allocation of GPU nodepools across tenants while preserving immutability in IAM role configuration for auditability. Current tenant lease allocation can drift when nodes are recreated or TF state is sharded, causing tenant-to-node affinity regressions and noisy autoscaler behavior. This PR introduces a new Terraform module that provides a deterministic `lease_index` allocator for GPU nodepools and an IAM role templating system that creates immutable role versions with automated rotation hooks.

What this PR does: 1) Adds module `tfmodules/gpu_lease_index` implementing a lease-index allocation strategy that: computes a stable index for tenant leases based on tenant-id hashing + ordered subnet slices; emits outputs consumed by the nodepool provisioning module to ensure stable assignment even during partial applies and state sharding. 2) Adds module `tfmodules/iam_role_template` which renders IAM role artifacts as immutable-versioned resources (role_v1, role_v2) and wires a controlled rotation mechanism via a small controller job (K8s Job manifest + preflight plan) that can be driven by `private-installer upgrade`. 3) Provides a migration tool `scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate` (dry-run + apply modes) to compute and persist lease_index mappings into a new state namespace, with safety checks and a --rollback snapshot option. 4) Adds tagging for GPU nodepool instances to surface lease_index and role_version for drift detection and reconciler diagnostics. 5) Updates Helm hooks in `charts/private-installer` to add post-upgrade validation for IAM rotations and nodepool tag presence.

Design notes: - Lease index derivation uses a stable HMAC(nonce=deployment-id, key=infra-hash) truncated to the pool size to ensure stable ordering across applies. The TF module exposes both `lease_index` and `lease_salt` so downstream modules can detect mismatches and trigger safe rebinds. - IAM role templating intentionally creates new role resources instead of in-place edits; the rotation controller sequences traffic to roles by annotating service accounts, running preflight checks, then promoting a new version, and finally deleting the old role only when safe. - The migration tool does not alter live node labels; it only writes the lease_index mapping into the TF state file and produces a reconciliation plan for the installer to apply.

Commits: ["a1b9f7c add gpu_lease_index module and unit tests", "b4d3e82 add iam_role_template module and rotation job manifest", "f7c2a10 add tfstate-lease-index-migrate script with dry-run", "e3f9b5a update helm chart hooks for post-upgrade validation", "c9d0aa4 add nodepool tagging and reconciler metrics", "d2f8f4b docs: migration and rollout notes"]

Files changed (high level): ["tfmodules/gpu_lease_index/main.tf", "tfmodules/gpu_lease_index/outputs.tf", "tfmodules/gpu_lease_index/README.md", "tfmodules/iam_role_template/main.tf", "tfmodules/iam_role_template/rotation-job.yaml", "scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate", "charts/private-installer/templates/post-upgrade-validation.yaml", "charts/private-installer/values.schema.json", "modules/nodepool/attach_tags.tf", "modules/nodepool/README.md", "docs/private-deploy/migration-lease-index.md", "tests/lease_index/unit_tests.go"]

Testing and CI: - Unit tests for lease-index math (Go) and TF unit-style checks included; added integration test to create a two-tenant private deployment and assert stable assignment across repeated applies with shuffled state shards. - CI initially failed due to a lint rule on HCL variable naming; fixed and re-ran. Final CI run: all checks passed (plan acceptance, fmt, terratest, unit).

Review thread summary: - Arun: requested explicit docs on operator runbook for manual rotation fallback; added `docs/private-deploy/iam-rotation-runbook.md` and a small CLI helper. - Sophie: asked to add a preflight check that verifies service account annotation propagation; added contract test and Helm hook. - Mateo: flagged potential permission escalation during rotation; updated IAM policies to require `iam:PassRole` only scoped to role_version resource names and added audit logging. - Reviewer approvals: Arun approved after runbook added, Sophie approved after tests; Mateo requested one follow-up (see TODO).

Migration plan and rollout: 1) Run `scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate --dry-run` in staging to preview mappings. 2) Apply mapping with `--apply` to write to a new state namespace (non-destructive) and run `private-installer upgrade --validate`. 3) Observe reconciler metrics: if any tenant reports lease mismatch, the reconciler will temporarily create a shadow nodepool and migrate workloads before deleting the old nodepool (safe by default). 4) IAM rotation: default is manual and gated; operator can run `private-installer iam rotate --role <template> --promote` which triggers the rotation job. A fully-automated rollout is behind a feature flag.

Rollback: - The migration tool produces a snapshot file that can be used to revert state; the `--rollback` flag re-applies the snapshot into TF backend. - IAM rotation includes a rollback path: the controller keeps the previous role until operator confirms traffic has drained.

Risk and mitigation: - Risk: mismatch between lease_index and live node labels during partial apply. Mitigation: tagging + reconciler detection; migration initially run in dry-run and then staged apply; daily reconciliation window during first week. - Risk: accidental role deletion. Mitigation: immutable role versions retained for 7 days by default and deletion requires explicit operator approval recorded in the runbook.

Follow-ups and TODO: - ENG-7460: automated promotion pipeline for IAM rotation (epic). - Add metrics for lease_index churn to SLO dashboards. - Lightweight UI in Console for lease mapping visualization (product ticket).

Merge outcome: merged via squash after approvals and CI green.

title: Introduce adaptive probe-reservoir with backpressure governor and staged rollback policy
preview:
Motivation: recent short-lived concurrency spikes and noisy tail latency events caused false positives in the canary detector and triggered unnecessary rollbacks during high-variance traffic windows. This PR introduces an adaptive probe-reservoir (a small, prioritized probe pool) that spaces synthetic probes, aggregates microburst samples, and engages a lightweight backpressure governor to prevent probe amplification under overload. The design also includes a staged rollback policy that gradually reduces traffic to the candidate in controlled steps when a real regression is detected, instead of an immediate full rollback, giving operators time to observe correlated signals and avoid oscillation.

What this changes (summary):
- New probe reservoir scheduler that stratifies probes by route and concurrency slice and maintains a FIFO/tokens reservoir for burst smoothing.
- Backpressure governor that temporarily throttles probe generation when host-level queue depth or GPU saturation exceeds thresholds (uses existing runtime metrics as inputs).
- Staged rollback state machine added to the rollout controller: WARN -> THROTTLE -> PARTIAL_ROLLBACK -> FULL_ROLLBACK. Each stage can be configured with time windows and metric gates.
- Observability additions: new metrics (probe_reservoir_size, reservoir_drain_rate, rollback_stage, backpressure_trigger_count) and dashboards/alerting snippets.
- Unit and integration tests for reservoir behavior, governor threshold transitions, and staged rollback transitions.

Checklist:
- [x] Design spec reviewed (linked in ENG-4312)
- [x] Unit tests covering core reservoir and governor logic
- [x] Integration test that simulates microburst traffic and verifies graceful degradation
- [x] Docs: operational runbook and dashboard additions
- [x] Backwards-compatible config flags (feature gated under "probe_reservoir.enabled")

Testing notes: Local perf harness ran with synthetic microbursts (10x baseline concurrency for 30s) and reduced false-positive canary signals from 6/day to 1/day in our canary lab. CI: lint, unit, and integration suites all passed. Staging rollout (redwood-canary) ran for 48 hours without triggering escalations.

title: chronicle-writer: compact onboarding abstracts and deterministic release zipper
preview: Context: onboarding churn and release-note noise slow cross-team reviews. Large profile uploads include verbose provenance and full sample dumps; downstream consumers only need a small, deterministic artifact to verify compatibility and pull release summaries. This PR introduces the 'chronicle-writer' pipeline which emits a compact, deterministic zip (the 'chronicle') containing: a lightweight onboarding abstract (machine + human friendly), a sampled provenance manifest (configurable seed & reservoir size), delta fingerprints for fast diffs, and a concise release index used by downstream syncers. Motivation: speed up reviews, reduce artifact size for audits, and enable deterministic release replays for regressions. What this PR changes: 1) chronicle service: new CLI and server handler to build deterministic zips from profile uploads; 2) compact-onboard-abstract: a templated, schema-backed one-page summary derived from model metadata + top-k perf tokens; 3) sampled-provenance: reservoir sampler integrated into the onboard pipeline with seed control to ensure reproducible samples; 4) zipper: deterministic packaging/ordering + SHA256 manifest and timestamping; 5) release-sync helpers: small client library to fetch and validate chronicles and apply delta patches. Compatibility notes: chronicles are opt-in at onboarding and enabled by default for curated workflows; they are additive and do not replace existing full-profile bundles. Checklist: - [x] CLI + server handlers implemented - [x] deterministic ordering + manifest hashing - [x] reservoir sampling with seed control - [x] tests for determinism under varying node counts - [x] basic docs and QuickStart snippet - [x] migration script to backfill chronicles for recent 30 days Rollout plan: merge behind feature flag chronicle.enabled=false, enable for curated bucket the next day, observe storage and review latency for 72h, then flip default for new uploads. Related work: ties to ENG-8842 (release UX) and the release zipper RFC (internal link).
